Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cicloturista-cerdanya/

Spectacular Pyrenean cyclotourist ride with stunning valley views and iconic climbs across Spain-France border.

### UCI Cyprus Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 63.4 mi | 6,562 ft | Paphos, Cyprus | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cyprus-gran-fondo/

Strong T2 UCI stage race - qualification + tourism excellence 3-day stage race from Pafos Castle Square:
- **Stage 1 (Fri 3 Apr)**: 28.6km ITT - uphill to 300m peak then descent
- **Stage 2 (Sat 09:00)**: 102km GF (15km neutral → 3 climbs: 8km@4%, 6.5km@6%, 13.5km@4.5% to 65km peak)
- **Stage 3 (Sun 08:00)**: 59km GF w/ 700m@9% climb + 45km medio shortcut[1][2] Non-negotiables: UCI road/time trial bikes only (1.3.023); Support cars exit before final 3km; No drafting in ITT - 25m rule

### Dartmoor Classic Sportive
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 107.5 mi | 8,858 ft | Dartmoor National Park, Devon, England | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/dartmoor-classic/

The Dartmoor Classic is the UK's premier moorland sportive, combining exceptional organization, iconic climbs, and genuine challenge in one of England's most dramatic landscapes. Dramatic moorland challenge combining demanding ascents with exhilarating descents across Dartmoor's most iconic roads. The event showcases the rugged beauty of Dartmoor National Park with sweeping views and world-class event organization. Signature challenge: The Grande route's 107-mile, 2,700m elevation gain challenge through Dartmoor's toughest climbs, culminating in the closed-road hill climb competition segment where riders compete for King/Queen of the Mountain honors. Non-negotiables: Closed-road hill climb competition segment on all routes with category prizes and King/Queen of the Mountain crowning; Comprehensive support infrastructure: feed stations, mechanical assistance, professional medical coverage, rider support vehicles; High-quality event jersey included with entry

### Fred Whitton Challenge — The Daddy of Them All
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 112 mi | 12,795 ft | Grasmere, Lake District, Cumbria, England, UK | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/fred-whitton-challenge/

112 miles. 3,900 metres. Seven passes. Hardknott at 33% on mile 96. Britain's hardest sportive, run for charity since 1999. There is no harder single-day cycling event in the British Isles. The Fred Whitton Challenge was the first sportive ever held in the UK, and after 27 editions nothing has surpassed it. The route strings together every major Lake District pass — Kirkstone, Honister, Newlands, Whinlatter, Cold Fell, Hardknott, Wrynose, and Blea Tarn — into 112 miles and 3,900 metres of climbing through a UNESCO World Heritage landscape. The numbers alone are formidable. What makes the Fred legendary is the sequencing. The first 90 miles soften you up. Then at mile 96, with your glycogen reserves exhausted and your legs carrying the accumulated damage of 3,000+ metres of climbing, you arrive at the foot of Hardknott Pass. The road tilts to 33%. Your rear wheel slips on the tarmac. Riders walk. The strong survive. Over the top, Wrynose and Blea Tarn deliver the double and triple blows that break whatever the first 96 miles left intact.

But the Fred is not just a hard ride — it is a memorial, a charity, and a community. Fred Whitton was a popular member of the Lakes Road Club who died in 1998 at fifty years old. His clubmates rode this route in his honour the following year and raised money for Macmillan Cancer Support. Twenty-seven years and £2,000,000 later, the event is still powered by over 350 volunteers, and every penny of the £200 charity donation goes directly to cancer nurses, air ambulances, mountain rescue, and youth cycling. The ballot entry system means places are coveted. The atmosphere at Grasmere on the morning of the Fred is unlike any other UK cycling event. This is not a brand activation with a sportive attached. This is a cycling club honouring a dead friend by running the hardest ride they could imagine, year after year, for charity. Yes if you want the defining climbing challenge in British cycling — the one every serious UK cyclist measures themselves against. Yes if 3,900 metres of climbing through the Lake District's most savage passes appeals to you. Yes if you value charity, community, and memorial over timing chips and podiums. Yes if you can handle Hardknott's 33% gradient on legs with 96 miles already in them. Skip if you need competitive structure, guaranteed entry, reliable weather, or closed roads. Non-negotiables: Low gearing: 34/34 or lower — a standard road cassette will not save you on Hardknott; Three training rides of 80+ miles with 2,000+ metres of climbing — at least one with a hard climb after mile 60; Wet-weather descending confidence on narrow, steep roads

### Granfondo Gavia e Mortirolo
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 84 mi | 13,123 ft | Ponte di Legno, Brescia, Lombardy, Italy | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gf-gavia-e-mortirolo/

The Mortirolo at 18%. The Gavia at 2,618m. Pantani's mountains compressed into 135 km of pure Italian Alpine brutality. Compact and devastating. At 135 km, this is not a test of endurance — it's a test of climbing power and altitude tolerance. The route wastes no time: the Mortirolo hits early with its 18% ramps, the Gavia climbs to 2,618m through thinning air, and the Tonale provides one final sustained effort before the return to Ponte di Legno. There is almost no flat. The climbing density (4,000m in 135 km) is among the highest of any sportive on Earth. Signature challenge: The Mortirolo at 18% followed by the Gavia at 2,618m. These two climbs — one defined by gradient, the other by altitude — create a combined challenge unlike anything else in amateur cycling. The Mortirolo destroys your legs; the Gavia destroys your lungs. The GF Gavia e Mortirolo is not the longest gran fondo, not the highest, and not the biggest. It is, kilometer for kilometer, the most brutal. The Mortirolo's 18% gradients reduce strong riders to survival mode — there is no 'riding tempo' on this climb, only suffering and stubbornness. The Gavia at 2,618m takes whatever the Mortirolo left and dissolves it in thin air. These are the mountains where Marco Pantani became il Pirata, where Andy Hampsten rode through a snowstorm in 1988. You don't ride this event for a medal or a time. You ride it to discover what's left of you after the Mortirolo takes everything you thought you had. Yes if you want the steepest sustained gradients in any amateur cycling event. Yes if the Giro d'Italia legends of Mortirolo and Gavia mean something to you. Skip if 18% gradients terrify you or your gearing doesn't go below 34x30. Skip if you need a large international field and polished logistics. Non-negotiables: Steep climbing specificity — repeated efforts at 10-15% gradient for 30+ minutes; Gearing check: 34x32 minimum, 34x34 recommended; Altitude preparation or acclimatization for 2,618m

### Granfondo Matildica
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 95.1 mi | 7,054 ft | Reggio Emilia, Emilia-Romagna, Italy | September | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-matildica/

Essential UCI qualifier for road enthusiasts: historic, hilly, and a steal at €55. Hilly Apennine roads with rolling terrain, shared bulk between distances; gran fondo adds loops including 4km climb at 5.5% (km53) and 11km at 3.5% (km60+). Signature challenge: Long false-flat climb after initial steeper ascent, building cumulative fatigue. Non-negotiables: UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier; Comprehensive race-day support; Affordable entry for high-quality event

### Granfondo Novi Sad
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 62.1 mi | 4,921 ft | Novi Sad, Serbia | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-novi-sad/

Circular route: neutral start via Rainbow Bridge along Danube to Beočin. **Suffering Zone 1**: 19km - 9km climb (4.5% avg, 8% max). Descent through Mala Remeta/Jazak to Vrdnik. **Suffering Zone 2**: 55km - 6km climb (3.8% avg, 7% max). 11km rolling Fruška Gora ridge (selective). Return descent. Medio splits at 62km. 2km flat sprint. Non-negotiables: UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier; Fruška Gora National Park vineyards & monasteries; City center start/finish

### Grinduro
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 60 mi | 7,800 ft | Multi-Location Global Series | — | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro/

Four timed segments scattered across 60 miles of Sierra Nevada dirt — race hard when it counts, cruise with friends when it doesn't. Grinduro flips gravel racing on its head - four brutal timed segments scattered across 60 miles of Sierra Nevada terrain, with social cruise sections between the suffering. You'll ride conversationally through spectacular mountain scenery, then absolutely torch yourself for 5-15 minutes when the clock starts ticking. Signature challenge: The format itself is the killer - you can't pace conservatively because only four segments count, but you can't go full gas because there are three more stages coming. It rewards complete riders who can climb, descend, and suffer repeatedly while managing effort across 60 miles of Sierra Nevada punishment. You need legitimate climbing legs for 7,800 feet and technical handling skills for those singletrack descents. The format saves you from pacing disasters but won't save you from being undertrained. Show up fit enough to go anaerobic for 10 minutes at a time across varied terrain, or you'll get shelled on every timed segment.

### Hærvejsløbet — The Viking Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 186 mi | 3,300 ft | Flensburg, Germany to Viborg, Denmark | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/haervejslobet/

Viking-fueled endurance test blending history, hills, and high-quality organization. Historic point-to-point road epic tracing the ancient Hærvejen ridge through Jutland's ice-age formed hills, blending cultural landmarks with relentless rolling terrain. Signature challenge: Crossing the German-Danish border early, then battling cumulative elevation and wind-swept ridges to the triumphant red carpet finish in Viborg.

### Haute Route Ventoux
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 168 mi | 24,606 ft | Bedoin, Provence, France | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/haute-route-ventoux/

Three summits of cycling's most legendary mountain in three days — the definitive Ventoux experience with professional stage-race support. A 3-day stage race centered entirely on Mont Ventoux — cycling's most legendary standalone mountain. Each stage radiates from Bedoin through the surrounding Provencal countryside before converging on the summit at 1,912m via a different ascent route. The course design is elegant in its simplicity: three days, three sides, one mountain. The climbing is relentless, the exposure above treeline is savage, and the cumulative fatigue of three consecutive summit finishes separates those who trained from those who hoped. Signature challenge: The cumulative summit assault: three ascents of Mont Ventoux (1,912m) in three days from three different sides. Each approach has distinct character, but all converge on the same exposed, wind-battered final 6 km of moonscape. By Stage 3, the climb you know is waiting breaks you mentally before the gradient breaks you physically. Mont Ventoux is cycling's most mythological mountain — a barren, windswept colossus that has broken professionals in the Tour de France and claimed the life of Tom Simpson in 1967. The Haute Route Ventoux takes this legendary climb and demands you do it three times in three days, each from a different side. 270 km. 7,500 meters of climbing. Three summit finishes at 1,912m on the exposed moonscape where the Mistral can gust past 90 km/h.

The Bedoin ascent is the classic: 21.5 km at 7.6% average, with a murderous 10 km stretch through the forest averaging 9-10%. The Malaucene side hits you immediately with 12% gradients before a brief mid-climb reprieve. The Sault approach lulls you with 26 km of gentle lavender-country climbing before the exposed final kilometers deliver the same savage summit as the other two sides. By Stage 3, your legs have climbed to 1,912m twice already. The third ascent is where character is revealed.

The Haute Route organization is world-class — feed stations, mechanical support, timing, post-stage meals, and daily rankings make this feel like a professional stage race for amateur riders. The field of 300-500 creates genuine camaraderie forged through shared suffering. Provence as a backdrop — vineyards, gorges, medieval villages — adds culture and cuisine to complement the climbing. This is not a race for beginners. It is a pilgrimage for serious cyclists who understand that Ventoux is not just a mountain — it is a test of everything you are. Ride this if Mont Ventoux is your cycling grail and you want all three ascents in a professionally supported format. Skip it if you are on a budget (EUR 600-900 entry), prefer flat or gravel terrain, or cannot commit to 3 consecutive days of serious climbing. If you just want to climb Ventoux once, ride it independently — it is a public road. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance for 60-90 minute efforts; Multi-day recovery capacity; Headwind and exposure strategy

### L'Étape Parma
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 83 mi | 9,186 ft | Parma, Emilia-Romagna, Italy | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-parma/

Tour de France-branded gran fondo in Parma's stunning food hills.

### LoToJa Classic — Logan to Jackson
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 203 mi | 9,800 ft | Logan, Utah to Teton Village, Wyoming | — | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/lotoja-classic/

America's longest one-day road race. Three states. Three passes. One unforgettable day. An odyssey across three states and three mountain passes. The front half punishes you with climbing; the back half tests whether anything remains. At 203 miles, LoToJa is not about any single climb or feature — it is about the accumulation of everything across an entire day. Signature challenge: Three mountain passes in the first 110 miles — then 90+ miles on depleted legs. The front-loaded climbing ensures that the 'flat' back half of the course is anything but easy. At mile 150, every false flat feels like a climb. At mile 180, you still have a marathon of cycling remaining. The signature challenge is not any single climb — it is the relentless accumulation of 203 miles. LoToJa is the American one-day classic that European cycling traditions inspired but the Mountain West made its own. Two hundred three miles from a Utah college town to the foot of the Tetons, across three states and three mountain passes, in a single day. It is not the hardest race in America. It is not the most famous. But it might be the most audacious — a race conceived in 1983 when two Logan cyclists decided that Americans deserved their own Paris-Roubaix and built it through sheer mileage. LoToJa is for riders who want more than a century ride — who want a genuine odyssey. If you can sustain moderate effort for 10-13 hours, have a reliable crew, and want a finishing story that few other events can match, this is your race. But be honest with yourself: 203 miles is a very long day, and the three mountain passes in the first half ensure you will face the final 90 miles on tired legs. Non-negotiables: Long ride progression to 7+ hour sustained efforts; Crew logistics planned and rehearsed; Mountain climbing block with sustained efforts at threshold

### Bob Cook Memorial Mt. Evans Hill Climb (Blue Sky Hill Climb)
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 27.4 mi | 6,590 ft | Idaho Springs, Colorado | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/mt-evans-hill-climb/

27 miles. 6,590 feet. Summit at 14,130 ft. The highest paved cycling race in North America since 1962. One direction: up. The Mt. Evans Hill Climb is 27.4 miles of continuous ascending from the town of Idaho Springs at 7,540 feet to the summit parking area at 14,130 feet. The gradient is never savage — averaging 4.5% with a maximum around 10% — but the relentlessness is the point. There are no descents, no flat recovery sections (save a brief respite at Echo Lake), and no escape from the altitude that builds with every pedal stroke. The course passes through three distinct life zones: montane forest, subalpine forest with ancient bristlecone pines, and finally barren alpine tundra where nothing grows and mountain goats outnumber cyclists. Above treeline at 11,500 feet, the landscape transforms into an exposed, wind-blasted moonscape. The final miles above 13,000 feet are ridden in a hypoxic fog where your body is operating on roughly 60% of sea-level oxygen. Signature challenge: The final 5 miles above Summit Lake. From 12,830 feet to 14,130 feet, exposed switchbacks climb through alpine nothing. The oxygen is gone. The trees are gone. The gradient is honest (5.7% average) but your body has been robbed of its ability to process it. This is where the Mt. Evans Hill Climb separates itself from every other cycling event on the continent. There is no higher paved road you can race a bicycle on in North America. That sentence is the entire sales pitch, and it is enough. Since 1962, cyclists have lined up in Idaho Springs to ride 27 miles straight up into oxygen debt, hypothermia risk, and the company of mountain goats. The race honors Bob Cook, a five-time winner who died at 23 — a reminder that the mountain does not care about your credentials. At the summit, you will stand at 14,130 feet with 40% less oxygen and 100% more perspective. The Mt. Evans Hill Climb is not the hardest race. It is not the longest. It is not the most prestigious. But it is the highest, and nothing else comes close. Yes if you want the most extreme altitude experience available in competitive cycling on paved roads. Yes if you have trained for altitude or can arrive early enough to acclimatize. Skip if you have altitude sickness history, need technical terrain variety, or want a mass-participation atmosphere. Non-negotiables: Altitude acclimatization — arrive in Colorado at least 3-5 days before the race; Sustained climbing endurance — 2-3 hour continuous climbing efforts in training; Cold-weather gear for July — arm warmers, vest, full-finger gloves, ear cover

### NorthCape 4000
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 2485 mi | 98,000 ft | Rovereto, Italy to North Cape, Norway | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/northcape-4000/

Ultimate self-supported European traverse from Rovereto to North Cape redefines endurance adventure.

### RAMROD — Ride Around Mount Rainier in One Day
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 154 mi | 10,000 ft | Enumclaw, Washington, USA | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/ramrod/

154 miles. 10,000 feet. One active volcano. The Pacific Northwest's defining cycling challenge since 1984. RAMROD is a circumnavigation. You ride all the way around an active volcano and come back to where you started. That simple premise — one loop, one mountain, one day — produces one of the most varied and rewarding days you will ever spend on a bicycle. The ride begins in the agricultural lowlands east of Tacoma, where Enumclaw sits at the Cascade foothills. Depending on the route edition, you may head counterclockwise toward the Nisqually Entrance and Paradise (the classic route) or clockwise toward Crystal Mountain and Cayuse Pass (the modern bypass). Either way, the terrain transitions dramatically: flat farmland gives way to rolling foothills, which give way to towering old-growth forest, which gives way to alpine passes where the air cools and the mountain dominates the sky. Stevens Canyon Road — when permitted — is the emotional heart of the ride: a 1930s-era park road through a glacial valley with stone bridges and tunnels. Cayuse Pass at mile 100+ is the physical heart: 8 miles at 6% when your legs have nothing left. The long descent back to Enumclaw in the Pacific Northwest's extended summer twilight is the reward. The mountain was always there. You rode all the way around it. Signature challenge: Cayuse Pass at mile 100. Eight miles at a constant 6% grade in afternoon sun with no shade, on legs that have already climbed 6,000-7,000 feet over 100 miles. Every RAMROD veteran knows the name. The rest stop halfway up Cayuse is the most grateful you will ever be for a cup of water and a folding chair. The climb is not technically difficult — it is 6% and relentless — but arriving at its base after 100 miles of riding around a volcano transforms a moderate climb into a test of will. Cayuse Pass is where RAMROD separates the people who trained for 150 miles from the people who trained for 100. Forty years ago, a cyclist named John Dixon convinced 51 other people to ride their bicycles all the way around Mount Rainier in a single day. Forty-one editions later, 800 people win a lottery each year for the privilege of doing the same thing. RAMROD is the Pacific Northwest condensed into a single day on a bicycle: old-growth forest, alpine passes, glacial rivers, quiet foothills towns, and a 14,411-foot active volcano that is always there — above the treeline, above the clouds, above everything. The ride starts in the dark of Enumclaw at 5 AM with breakfast cooked by the local Rotary club. It climbs to Paradise through cathedral forests with the mountain overhead. It crosses Stevens Canyon on 1930s stone bridges through a glacial valley. It grinds up Cayuse Pass at mile 100 on legs that stopped cooperating 20 miles ago. And it returns to Enumclaw 150 miles later, in the long Pacific Northwest twilight, with the mountain still watching. RAMROD is not the hardest ride in America. It is not the longest. But it may be the most complete — a full orbit of something genuinely magnificent, earned one pedal stroke at a time. Yes if you want to circumnavigate an active volcano in a single day on one of America's most storied cycling routes. Yes if 150+ miles through national park alpine passes, old-growth forest, and glacial valleys is your idea of a perfect day. Yes if you value community tradition over timing chips and age-group podiums. Enter the lottery every year and volunteer when you can. Skip if you need competitive structure, technical terrain, or a ride shorter than 150 miles. Skip if you cannot handle uncertainty — the route changes annually and the event's future depends on National Park Service permitting decisions. Non-negotiables: At least two training rides of 120+ miles with 6,000+ feet of climbing in a single day; Fueling strategy validated for 10-15 hours of sustained riding — 250-300 calories per hour minimum; Layering system for 30-degree temperature swings: arm warmers, vest, light gloves packed and accessible

### Sea Otter Ciclobrava
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 87 mi | 6,562 ft | Girona, Costa Brava, Spain | September | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/sea-otter-ciclobrava/

Girona's paved party packs festival punch without the pro pain. Paved poetry through Girona's Pyrenees-Mediterranean embrace: low-traffic roads wind from urban start to coastal climbs and charming villages. Non-competitive freedom lets you savor Vuelta passes like Romanyà and La Ganga at your pace. Festival finish at Devesa promenade caps the joyride amid cheering crowds. Signature challenge: The quadruple-pass onslaught of Sant Grau d’Ardenya, Romanyà, La Ganga, and Montjuïc on the 140km—Vuelta-flavored beasts strung like pearls through Costa Brava, blending climb-drama with sea-view salvation for unforgettable road romance. Sea Otter Ciclobrava nails the non-competitive dream: 140km of Girona's smoothest paved passes amid Costa Brava splendor, all wrapped in Sea Otter's electric expo energy. Pros train these roads for a reason—low-traffic gems like Romanyà deliver Vuelta vibes without the pain train. It's cycling vacation disguised as a sportive, perfect for joyriders craving community over contention. Weak on gravel grit and elite depth, but crushes experience and value. Yes if you're a road enthusiast craving Sea Otter expo magic, Girona climbs, and zero-pressure pedaling. Skip if gravel's your gospel or you demand deep competitive fields. Non-negotiables: Base build to 100km+ weekly with 1500m+ elevation; Practice low-traffic descents and group riding

### Struggle Dales
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 109 mi | 9,430 ft | Harrogate, North Yorkshire, England | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/struggle-dales/

Struggle Dales is one of the UK's most challenging and prestigious sportives, offering ambitious road cyclists an unforgettable test of endurance across the stunning Yorkshire Dales. Struggle Dales is one of the UK's hardest sportives, set in the beautiful but brutal Yorkshire Dales landscape. The event takes riders over stunning yet unforgiving terrain through the Yorkshire Dales National Park, featuring seven iconic climbing challenges that have earned the route cult status among ambitious road cyclists nationwide. Signature challenge: Park Rash stands as the most renowned climb on the route, recognized as a defining feature of the Struggle Dales experience and a bucket-list challenge for serious road cyclists.

### Tartu Rattaralli
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 81.4 mi | 3,858 ft | Tartu, South Estonia, Estonia | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tartu-rattaralli/

Fast, flawless Baltic UCI ticket-puncher. Deceptively flat-fast rocket through South Estonia's undulating greens. Grupettos hammer smooth roads with moto escort; positioning is king amid endless micro-hills. City center roar caps the high-speed chess match. Signature challenge: Relentless pacing on 'flat' terrain—every watt and wheel counts. Flying feeds let you stay glued; one lapse and you're dropped. Tartu Rattaralli is Europe's grizzled vet still punching above weight with UCI fire. Flying feeds and smooth speed turn qualifiers into groupo spectacles where positioning trumps climbing. Tartu's vibe—student buzz, medieval charm—makes it a cycling holiday, not just a race. 40+ years prove it's bulletproof; kids' races add soul most events fake. Yes if you're age-group hunting Worlds quals on flat power. Skip if vert-junkie or gravel purist—too tame. Non-negotiables: 12-15hr training weeks with threshold intervals; Sprint finish drills

### Trek Across Maine

Lung-loving legend of supported Maine miles. Three-day loop from Pineland Farms slices central Maine's rolling heart: lupine-choked fields, glassy lakes, river glimpses, and woodsy whispers. Signature challenge: The three-day unsupported-feel grind: 60-mile days blend hills, heat, and heart for lung legacy. Overnights recharge with brews and bands, but cumulative fatigue defines the trek. Charity fire keeps wheels turning. Trek Across Maine nails the charity multi-day formula: 180 scenic miles, rolling Maine charm, and college-camp vibes with brews and bands. Lung legacy since '85 elevates it beyond mere miles—ride for health heroes while feasting on pizza and lupine views. Virtual flex and kid-friendly tandems make it inclusive gold. Prestige lacks pro punch, but experience overflows. This ain't racing; it's righteous rolling. Yes if you're fundraising for good, love multi-day parties, or seek family-inclusive road joy. Skip if elite prestige or gravel grit calls—or hills bore you. Non-negotiables: 50-mile rides weekly; $550 fundraising locked

A legendary Mont Ventoux summit finish in Provence with world-class organization and unforgettable scenery—a true bucket-list gran fondo. A legendary Provençal gran fondo centered on Mont Ventoux, Europe's most iconic climb. The route traverses stunning landscapes—lavender fields, vineyards, Gorges de la Nesque, and quaint villages—before culminating in a dramatic summit finish at 1,912 m. The event emphasizes both sporting challenge and cultural immersion in Provence. Signature challenge: Mont Ventoux summit finish via Bédoin (Granfondo) or Sault (Mediofondo/Piccolofondo). The final ascent is renowned as one of France's most demanding climbs, combining sustained gradient, wind exposure, and psychological challenge of the barren, rocky summit. ### Giro Sardegna Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 81 mi | 8,530 ft | Siniscola, Sardinia, Italy | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/giro-sardegna-gravel/

Mediterranean gravel with UCI points that actually feels like a vacation until the dirt sections remind you why your watts matter. 130 km, 2,600 m. 75% gravel, 25% paved. Sardinian mountains—rolling terrain, coastal views. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 130 km + 2,600 m elevation + May warmth. Sardinian mountains Mediterranean views. Giro Sardegna Gravel is Sardinian mountains—130 km, 2,600 m elevation. Founded ~2020. 75% gravel, 25% paved. Sardinian mountains—rolling terrain, Mediterranean coastal views. 500-700 riders. Siniscola, Sardinia (coastal town). May spring conditions (18-28°C). Affordable entry (€90-€130 / $100-$145). If you want Sardinian island gravel with Mediterranean views and affordable entry, this is it. If you need local/domestic, skip it. International destination race. If you can hold threshold power on loose surfaces and don't mind mixing beach starts with proper gravel circuits, this delivers. The 50km circuit format means your pacing mistakes get amplified—show up undertrained for those dirt sections and the second lap becomes survival mode. Solid choice if you want UCI World Series points without the full sufferfest of most qualifiers.

### Granfondo Tre Valli Varesine
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 78.8 mi | 8,202 ft | Varese, Lombardy, Italy | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-tre-valli-varesine/

A prestigious UCI World Series qualifier with legitimate climbing and Italian cycling heritage, but premium costs and modest field depth limit universal appeal. The 127 km grandfondo follows the legendary pro Tre Valli Varesine route through pre-alpine terrain bordering three lakes (Lugano, Maggiore, Varese). The course alternates between sustained climbs and technical descents on fully paved, closed roads through charming pre-alpine villages. Starting and finishing in Varese city center, the route features an immediate 12 km ascent to Alpe Tedesco (the hardest and highest point), followed by technical descents, repeated climbs of Morosolo and Casciago, and a final uphill finish. The 2,500m elevation gain is distributed throughout, preventing flat recovery sections and maintaining pressure for 5+ hours. This is a serious climbing course disguised as a scenic lake tour. Signature challenge: The 2,500 meters of elevation gain distributed across 127 km creates relentless climbing without flat recovery sections. Unlike single-summit gran fondos, Tre Valli Varesine demands sustained climbing fitness and mental resilience across multiple ascents. The opening 12 km to Alpe Tedesco immediately establishes the course's serious nature, and repeated climbs of Morosolo and Casciago create a pattern of suffering that separates serious cyclists from casual riders. This is climbing volume and technical descent skill, not a scenic recreational ride. Granfondo Tre Valli Varesine trades on genuine cycling heritage and UCI World Series credentials to justify premium positioning. The 127 km course with 2,500m climbing delivers real athletic challenge on closed Italian roads through pre-alpine terrain. Organization by SC Alfredo Binda and the 2018 World Championships pedigree elevate this beyond typical gran fondos. However, unspecified costs, modest field depth, and variable fall weather prevent this from being an obvious must-do. This is a race for cyclists seeking UCI legitimacy and Italian cycling culture, not casual recreational riders. Race this if you're targeting UCI World Championships qualifiers, value cycling heritage and European authenticity, or want to ride the 2018 Worlds route. Skip if you're budget-conscious (Northern Italy is expensive), prefer larger international fields, or want guaranteed warm-weather conditions—October in Lombardy is unpredictable. Non-negotiables: Build sustained climbing fitness to 2,500m elevation gain capacity; Practice technical descending on closed roads or similar terrain; Develop nutrition strategy for 5+ hour efforts in cool weather

### Grinduro France
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 62 mi | 6,562 ft | Xonrupt-Longemer, Vosges, France | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro-france/

Grinduro's inaugural European outing trades California dust for Vosges forests, keeping the party-to-pain ratio intact. Enduro format: 4 timed descent stages (technical singletrack), untimed climbs. 62 miles, 6,562 ft. Vosges mountains: steep, forested, technical. Rewards MTB descending, allows social climbing. Signature challenge: Technical singletrack. MTB skills required. Crashes possible. Grinduro France is the French edition of the international festival-race series. Same format as Germany (untimed climbs, 4 timed descents), same party atmosphere (live music, camping, expo), but French: wine instead of beer, Vosges instead of Eifel, French food instead of German. New venue in 2024 (moved to Vosges from Monts du Lyonnais) brings stunning mountain scenery. Most riders prioritize experience over results. Technical descents reward MTB skills but are optional—untimed climbs allow social riding. If you loved Grinduro Germany but prefer French wine and mountain views, this is it. If you can handle 100 kilometers of varied terrain without crying about gravel bike handling, this works. The 2,000 meters of climbing spread across French forest roads won't destroy you, but the mix of surfaces rewards riders who can adapt rather than specialists. Show up for the weekend festival vibe, not pure racing—Grinduro's DNA is equal parts suffering and beer.

### Grinduro Germany
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 62 mi | 6,562 ft | Hellenthal, Eifel, Germany | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro-germany/

A weekend festival disguised as a gravel race where your descending skills matter more than your FTP. Unique enduro format: 4 timed descent stages (technical singletrack/doubletrack), all climbs untimed. Rewards MTB descending skills, allows social climbing. 62 miles, 6,562 ft elevation. Eifel volcanic landscape—German forest roads and technical descents. Signature challenge: First technical test. Sets the tone. MTB skills required. Loose, rooty, rocky. Grinduro Germany is what happens when you cross a gravel race with a music festival and MTB enduro. The format is unique: only 4 descent stages are timed (technical singletrack), while all climbs are untimed transport stages. You can climb at social pace, chat, stop for photos—then pin it on the timed descents if you want. Most riders are here for the festival: live music, beer garden, camping, expo. The racing is secondary. The Eifel region is stunning—volcanic landscape, German forest roads, near the Belgian border. If you want a chill weekend with friends, amazing scenery, and some optional racing—Grinduro delivers. If you want a serious gravel race, look elsewhere. If you can handle technical singletrack descents and care more about German beer than race times, this is perfect. The untimed climbs mean fitness matters less than bike handling—bring 42mm tires, a dropper post, and zero ego. Skip it if you want pure gravel racing or hate crowds partying while you're trying to suffer.

### L'Etape Costa Rica by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 80.8 mi | 8,202 ft | Cartago, Cartago Province, Costa Rica | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-costa-rica/

Tour spirit erupts on Irazú—rookie rocket with global glow Paved volcanic rollercoaster from Cartago shadows Irazú's brooding mass—steep ramps pierce cloud forests, descents carve lush canyons. Race route doubles down on climbs for Tour-true brutality; Ride eases in. Closed roads let amateurs chase polka-dots amid jungle roars. Signature challenge: Irazú Volcano ascent—2,500m cumulative near 11,000ft peak redefines tropical climbing. Pavement belies the hypoxic punch; foggy rims and jungle heat forge Tour legends from amateurs. L'Etape Costa Rica blasts Tour de France fever into Irazú's fiery jaws—rookie guts with pro polish. 130km of paved pain amid jungle drama crushes souls while jerseys crown kings. Central America's answer to Alps envy: cheaper, wilder, sweatier. Debut vibes scream breakout star; skip if you fear humidity hell. Yes if you crave Tour jerseys amid volcanoes and tropical chaos—perfect for adventure roadies. Skip if humidity horrors or logistics laziness plague you; wait for 2027 maturity. Non-negotiables: 8+ hour epic rides with 2,000m+ vert; Acclimatization ride at 2,000m+

### L'Étape Slovakia by Tour de France

T2 Urban start through Bratislava center, historical peripheral areas, Malé Karpaty foothill villages, **Suffering Zone: Pezinok vineyard climbs**, Carpathian Forest Park peaks to iconic Kamzík finish. **Suffering Zones:** Vineyard rollers (technical descents), final Carpathian peak grind to Kamzík. Non-negotiables: Official Tour de France L'Etape Series event; Professional organization and timing; Peter & Martin Velits as ambassadors

### Mammoth Tuff
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 100 mi | 7,500 ft | Mammoth Lakes, California | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/mammoth-tuff/

Altitude meets volcanic attitude in California's most honest sufferfest. 100 miles of High Sierra alpine gravel. 7,500 ft elevation, 7,880-8,000 ft altitude. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Smooth fast roads. Technical rating: 2/5. Altitude is the defining challenge. Signature challenge: Altitude at 8,000 ft. Smooth roads mean high speeds burn energy faster. Sea-level riders suffer. Mammoth Tuff is a UCI Gravel World Series qualifier at 8,000 feet. 100 miles of smooth High Sierra gravel. Altitude is the challenge—7,880-8,000 ft throughout means 30% less oxygen. If you're sea-level, arrive 3-5 days early or suffer. Course isn't technical (2/5), it's fast and smooth. Mammoth Lakes is expensive resort town. June Lake Loop (mile 40-60) is stunning. UCI status brings competitive field (Keegan Swenson, Lauren De Crescenzo). If you want High Sierra scenery with altitude suffering, this is it. Show up fit or the 8,000-foot starting elevation will expose every training shortcut you took. The 7,500 feet of climbing over 105 miles isn't negotiable, and volcanic gravel punishes both your legs and your equipment. This isn't a scenic tour—it's a legitimate training ground that's produced Unbound winners for a reason.

### Nordic Chase Gravel Edition
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 373 mi | 26,247 ft | Copenhagen, Denmark to Oslo, Norway | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/nordic-chase-gravel/

Three countries, 100 hours, and enough Scandinavian gravel to remind you why self-supported ultras don't negotiate. ~600 km, ~8,000 m. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Multi-day stage race (4 stages). Copenhagen to Oslo. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: Multi-day fatigue (4 days, 600 km). Supported but cumulative. Nordic Chase Gravel Edition is multi-day Scandinavian adventure—Copenhagen to Oslo, ~600 km over 4 stages. Founded ~2019. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Supported stage race (hotels, meals included in €400-€600 entry). 200-300 riders. 4 stages ~150km each. Scandinavian countryside—rolling terrain, coastal sections. June summer conditions (15-25°C). If you want multi-day European adventure with support, this is it. If you want single-day or budget race, skip it. Expensive but all-inclusive. You need serious self-supported experience and the mental fortitude for 100-hour efforts across three countries. The 60-80% gravel through Nordic forests isn't technical, but the distance and isolation will expose every weakness in your preparation and gear. Show up undertrained and you'll be arranging your own expensive extraction from rural Sweden.

### Oregon Trail Gravel Grinder
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 375 mi | 35,000 ft | Central Oregon (multiple towns) | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/oregon-trail-gravel/

The Grand Tour of American gravel—five days of Oregon's best, compressed into one transformative experience. Five distinct stages showcasing Oregon's diversity—high desert, forests, mountains, and everything between. Signature challenge: The cumulative nature. Any single stage is manageable. Five consecutive days of 60-80+ miles with major climbing is a different animal. Oregon Trail is for those who find single-day races limiting. The stage format creates different demands—recovery, consistency, pacing across days. It's closer to bikepacking than racing, with timing chips. Yes if you're ready for multi-day racing and want to experience gravel at its deepest. Not if you can't commit the time or prefer the intensity of one-day events.

### The Accursed Race (TAR)
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 994 mi | 121,391 ft | Albania/Montenegro/Bosnia and Herzegovina/Kosovo | May | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-accursed-race/

### TORTOUR
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 621 mi | 45,932 ft | Schaffhausen, Switzerland | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tortour/

1,000 km non-stop across Switzerland. Alpine passes by starlight. The world's ultimate ultra-endurance cycling test. The TORTOUR 1000 route traverses Switzerland in a massive loop, changing year to year but always incorporating multiple Alpine passes, Swiss lowland roads, and urban transitions. The character is defined not by any single climb or descent but by the accumulation: 1,000 km of terrain changes, day-to-night-to-day temperature cycles, the psychological battle of sustained effort, and the Swiss Alps appearing on the horizon when your body is already finished. The route takes riders through multiple cantons, language regions, and climate zones. It is Switzerland distilled into a 48-hour continuous experience. Signature challenge: The second night. After 24+ hours and 500+ km of continuous riding, darkness falls again and the body demands sleep. Pushing through the second night — often on Alpine terrain — is the defining challenge of the TORTOUR 1000. Everyone can ride the first 500 km. The TORTOUR is decided in the dark hours. The TORTOUR is not a bike race. It is a 1,000 km argument with yourself about whether you can keep pedaling through the night, over Alpine passes, across an entire country, for 48 straight hours. The world's largest multi-day non-stop ultra-endurance cycling event takes everything Switzerland throws at you — 12,000m of climbing, 30-degree temperature swings, complete darkness, Alpine dawn — and compresses it into a single continuous effort.

Solo unsupported is the purest form: you, your bike, and whatever you can carry for 1,000 km. Solo with support gives you a crew car and checkpoint assistance. Teams of 2-6 relay the distance. Every format is punishing. The TORTOUR 1000 solo finisher has earned something that no one-day sportive can replicate.

The 2026 edition returns to Schaffhausen, the event's birthplace, after six years in the Zurich canton. The prologue, pasta party, and finisher's brunch return to the original format. This is a homecoming for an event that has grown into the defining ultra-cycling challenge in Europe. Yes if ultra-endurance cycling is your discipline and you want to test yourself against 1,000 km of Swiss terrain in 48 hours. Yes if the idea of Alpine passes at 3 AM excites rather than terrifies you. Skip if you have never ridden through the night or covered more than 300 km in a single effort. Skip if the budget (CHF 3,000-5,000 all-in) is prohibitive. Non-negotiables: Multiple 400+ km training rides with night riding segments — minimum 20 hours continuous; Support crew logistics finalized — vehicle, driver rotation, checkpoint strategy; Night riding equipment tested — lights, reflective gear, warm layers for Alpine passes at 3 AM

### Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 205 mi | 26,247 ft | Les Saisies, Haute-Savoie, France | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-du-mont-blanc-cyclo/

Circumnavigate Mont-Blanc through France, Switzerland, and Italy. 330 km. 8,000m of climbing. Dawn to dusk. One mountain. Three countries. Epic in scale, relentless in execution. The 330 km route is a circumnavigation — you are literally riding around a mountain massif, and the sense of circling Mont-Blanc drives the entire experience. The six major cols are not stacked as brutally as the Marmotte or Gavia events — there are valley recovery sections between climbs — but the sheer accumulation of climbing over 15+ hours creates a unique kind of attrition. The middle section through Switzerland and Italy, with the Great St. Bernard as the high point, is the psychological crux. The Cormet de Roselend as the final major col, when 260 km are in the legs, is the physical crux. Signature challenge: The 330 km distance with 8,000m of climbing through three countries creates a unique attrition challenge. No single climb is as brutal as the Mortirolo or as iconic as Alpe d'Huez, but the accumulated effect of 6+ cols over 15+ hours, with the Great St. Bernard at 2,469m as the high point after 130+ km, is the defining challenge. The Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo is not for the majority of cyclists. It is for the minority who have looked at the map, seen that you can ride around Western Europe's highest mountain through three countries in a single day, and thought: yes. 330 km. 8,000 meters. Six major cols. Dawn to dusk. France, Switzerland, Italy, and back to France. The Great St. Bernard Pass at 2,469m is a crossing that Napoleon used to invade Italy — you're doing it on a road bike with 200 km already in your legs. This is not a sportive. This is a geographical statement. Yes if you have deep ultra-endurance fitness and want the most epic single-day cycling adventure in Europe. Yes if riding through three countries around Western Europe's highest peak speaks to your sense of journey. Skip if you haven't completed at least one 200+ km mountain sportive. Skip if the logistics of a 15-hour day across three countries overwhelm rather than excite you. Non-negotiables: Ultra-endurance base: at least three 200+ km rides with 3,000m+ climbing; 15-hour nutrition strategy tested and refined; Clothing strategy for 5C-30C range including fast transitions

### Tour of the Gila
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 334 mi | 15,000 ft | Silver City, Grant County, New Mexico, USA | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-of-the-gila/

America's only UCI stage race — open to amateurs. The Gila Monster at 7,400 ft is waiting. Multi-day stage racing at altitude through the Gila Wilderness. Each stage is a distinct test — the ITT sets the opening gaps, the road stages include genuine mountain climbs on narrow wilderness roads, and the criterium rewards tactical racing. The Gila Monster road race is the queen stage: 69 miles for amateurs with 5,200 ft of climbing, finishing at 7,436 ft on a narrow, winding mountain road through ponderosa pine forest. Signature challenge: The Gila Monster — 10.3 miles of narrow wilderness mountain road finishing at 7,436 ft. The only summit finish in American amateur stage racing that rivals European HC climbs for difficulty and atmosphere. Tour of the Gila is the most unique amateur road racing opportunity in the United States. It's the only place an amateur can pin on a number and race a UCI stage race course — the same climbs, the same roads, the same New Mexico wilderness that the professional peloton tackles. The Gila Monster climb is a genuine mountain summit finish that rivals anything in European professional cycling for atmosphere and difficulty. Silver City is remote, rugged, and authentically Western. This is not a corporate gran fondo with a finish-line festival — it's real stage racing in real wilderness. Yes if you want the unique experience of racing a UCI stage race as an amateur. Yes if you can handle altitude and remote logistics. Skip if you want a mass-participation gran fondo atmosphere. Non-negotiables: Stage race fitness — ability to recover between racing days; Altitude acclimatization or pre-adaptation strategy; USA Cycling license and category classification

### Whiskey Off-Road
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 50 mi | 6,523 ft | Prescott, Arizona | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/whiskey-off-road/

The Southwest's 20-year MTB institution where Skull Valley tests your suffering and Whiskey Row rewards your survival. A greatest-hits tour of Prescott National Forest combining technical singletrack, flowing trails, exposed fire road grinding, and tactical racing. You start on historic Whiskey Row, climb through granite formations, suffer through Skull Valley, flow through Spence Basin, and navigate technical descents when your legs are cooked. Every type of MTB terrain represented. Signature challenge: Skull Valley—a 12-mile exposed fire road climb at the halfway mark. 2,500-2,700ft of gain when you're already depleted. Documented 102F readings in hot years. Where cramping becomes universal and mental fortitude separates finishers from DNFs. The Whiskey Off-Road is the Southwest's premier mountain bike festival—a 20-year institution that combines genuine physical challenge with exceptional event production. Skull Valley will humble you. The technical sections demand MTB skills. But the weekend package—historic Whiskey Row, live music, expo, community—elevates it beyond just a race. It's what happens when an event organizer spends two decades perfecting their craft. Yes if you want a world-class MTB festival experience with genuine physical challenge. Yes if you appreciate 20 years of event refinement. No if you're a gravel purist uncomfortable on technical terrain. No if you need budget-friendly entry.

### Chequamegon MTB
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 40 mi | 4,000 ft | Cable, Wisconsin (Chequamegon National Forest) | September | mtb
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/chequamegon-mtb/

Forty miles of Northwoods power suffering disguised as a mountain bike race that somehow became a gravel institution. 40 miles, 4,000 ft. 60% forest roads, 40% singletrack. Technical MTB terrain. Mass start chaos. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: Mass start chaos (3,000 riders) + OO section technical singletrack. Chequamegon MTB is legendary—40+ years (founded 1983), 3,000+ riders, mass start chaos. Life Time Grand Prix race (pro field: Keegan Swenson, Payson McElveen). 40 miles, 4,000 ft. 60% forest roads, 40% singletrack. Technical MTB terrain—roots, rocks, OO section legendary. Cable small Wisconsin town. Mid-September fall conditions (50-70°F). If you want MTB history, mass start chaos, and LTGP glory, this is it. If you want gravel or dropbar, skip it. MTB required. Show up fit or don't show up at all—the Seeley Fire Tower climb doesn't care about your weekend warrior training plan. You need sustained power for constant rollers and the mental fortitude to handle September weather that swings from dust storms to mud festivals. If you can hold 250+ watts for three hours while navigating lottery systems and tourist crowds, this delivers old-school racing credibility.

### Cycling Shimanami 2026
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 43.5 mi | 1,300 ft | Imabari, Ehime Prefecture to Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cycling-shimanami/

Japan's most prestigious international cycling event offering unparalleled logistics, scenery, and cultural immersion across the Seto Inland Sea. A scenic, relatively flat island-hopping route across nine iconic bridges spanning the Seto Inland Sea. The Shimanami Kaido connects six islands (Oshima, Omishima, Hakatajima, Innoshima, Ikuchi-jima, and Mukaishima) between Imabari on Shikoku Island and Onomichi in Hiroshima Prefecture. Dedicated blue-line cycling paths, 150+ rest oases, and comprehensive aid stations with local food and cultural experiences characterize the route. The event allows cyclists unique access to the normally car-only expressway main road, creating an unforgettable atmosphere with closed roads, cheering locals, and photographers lining the route. Signature challenge: The nine bridge crossings themselves, particularly the longest bridge onto Shikoku Island, which require sustained effort but reward with spectacular Seto Inland Sea vistas. The optional Shiratakiyama climb offers the most significant elevation challenge with stone kami (gods) statues lining the ascent. Non-negotiables: Participants must stay behind staff guides on designated courses; Participants must maintain forward direction; intentional course deviation results in forced retirement and transport to finish; Participants must pass each checkpoint by set closing times or be directed to retire

### Gran Fondo Jordan
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 47.2 mi | 4,921 ft | Dead Sea, Jordan | January | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-jordan/

A geographically stunning and physically brutal three-day climbing challenge that rewards serious cyclists willing to travel. Gran Fondo Jordan is a three-day stage race that weaponizes geography. The Dead Sea's position as Earth's lowest point becomes the launching pad for sustained climbing that tests every system in your body. Stage 1 is a deceptively simple time trial along the lake—flat but windy, a tactical opener. Stages 2 and 3 are climbing masterclasses: relentless ascents with variable gradient patterns, steep sections that demand pacing discipline, and descents that offer brief respite before the next push. The course is fully paved but feels raw and exposed, with Dead Sea views that are simultaneously beautiful and humbling as you suffer upward. This is climbing-focused cycling at its most unforgiving. Signature challenge: The 30 km Mount Panorama climb on Stage 3 is the event's defining challenge. Divided into three steep steps (first 5-6 km steep, second steep section after 10 km, third steep part at km 18), with moderate recovery sections and even short downhills between them, this climb demands tactical pacing and mental toughness. Riders must manage their effort across three distinct steep sections while managing fatigue from the previous day's climbing. The final 6 km flatten out slightly, but by then most riders are operating on empty. This is climbing that breaks riders mentally and physically. Gran Fondo Jordan is a rare breed: a genuinely distinctive cycling event that justifies the logistical effort to reach it. The elevation profile is legitimately brutal, the location is unforgettable, and the UCI credentials are real. This isn't a mass-participation gran fondo—it's a curated challenge for serious cyclists willing to travel. The small field creates intimacy but limits competitive depth. If you're seeking a cycling adventure with genuine geographic and physical challenge, this delivers. If you want a casual weekend ride with thousands of peers, look elsewhere. Yes if you're a strong climber seeking a unique, UCI-sanctioned challenge with genuine adventure and don't mind international travel logistics. Skip if you prefer mass-participation events, casual weekend rides, or budget-friendly racing. Non-negotiables: Build sustained climbing fitness with 4-6 weeks of hill repeats and long climbs; Complete at least one 3-4 hour climbing-focused training ride with 1,000+ meters of elevation; Arrive 2-3 days early for acclimatization and course reconnaissance

### Gran Fondo Vosges
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 108.7 mi | 9,495 ft | La Bresse Hohneck ski station, Vosges, France | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-vosges/

T2: Premier UCI Gran Fondo qualifier with exceptional length and scenic suffering in accessible European mountains. Starts La Bresse-Hohneck → Lispach lake → **Suffering Zone 1: La Croix des Moinats (891m, early steep)** → **Haut du Tôt (866m, 5.5km sections >10%)** → Col des Ecureuils (678m) → **Col de Haux Jacques (605m, mid-race grind)** → Col du Champdray (733m) → **Suffering Zone 2: Col des Feignes (993m, 10km longest climb)** → 9km finish climb. 3 feed stations + pasta party.[1][2] Non-negotiables: UCI qualification rules (age/gender specific); Mountain weather prep (variable May conditions); Climbing legs for 175km/2900m+

### Granfondo La Guadeloupe
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 77.1 mi | 9,459 ft | Capesterre-Belle-Eau, Guadeloupe | December | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-la-guadeloupe/

### UCI Gravel Suisse
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 68 mi | 11,300 ft | Aigle / Villars-sur-Ollon, Switzerland | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-fondo-switzerland/

The UCI's backyard playground where 3,300 meters of climbing separates tourists from gravel racers. Starting at cycling's global headquarters in Aigle, this course lulls you through 30 miles of Rhone Valley false confidence before unleashing 3,300 meters of vertical punishment. The second half abandons civilization for alpine forest tracks and 20% wooden ladders disguised as bike paths. It's less gravel race, more high-altitude survival exam with a fancy UCI stamp. Signature challenge: The Route des Chaux section after mile 49 strips away any illusion that this is a normal gravel race. Twenty percent pitches on technical forest tracks at 5,000+ feet elevation turn bikes into anchors and legs into concrete. The thing nobody tells you: it's not just steep, it's steep when you're already cooked from the altitude and previous 2,000 meters of climbing. You need legitimate climbing legs and a gravel bike that can handle 20% grades on loose surfaces. The second half destroys weekend warriors—if you can't sustain power on steep off-road climbs above 1,500 meters, the Route des Chaux will expose you. Come for the prestige and alpine scenery, but expect to earn every meter of that Col de la Croix finish.

### Gunni Grinder
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 120 mi | 10,000 ft | Gunnison, Colorado | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gunni-grinder/

High-altitude gravel that separates your actual fitness from your sea-level delusions. High-altitude mountain gravel with sustained climbs and big descents. 10,000 ft elevation gain over 120 miles at 7,700-10,000 ft base. Altitude is the defining challenge—oxygen is 25-30% thinner than sea level. Cochetopa Pass (miles 30-45) is a 15-mile sustained grind. High alpine sections (miles 50-70) max out altitude. Taylor Canyon rollers (miles 85-105) finish with relentless punchy climbs. This is a legitimate mountain challenge, not rolling gravel. Signature challenge: 15 miles of sustained climbing at 8,000+ ft elevation. Not steep, but relentless. Altitude makes every pedal stroke harder. Just keep spinning. Gunni Grinder is high-altitude mountain gravel done right. This isn't a flat power race—it's a climbing test at thin oxygen. You start at 7,700 ft (already higher than most people live) and max out near 10,000 ft. If you're from low elevation, your FTP will drop 10-15%. Your breathing will be labored. Your bonk will come faster. The Cochetopa Pass Road climb (miles 30-45) is a 15-mile grind that just keeps going—not steep, but relentless at altitude. The high alpine sections (miles 50-70) are exposed, windy, and stunning—above treeline with panoramic Rocky Mountain views. The Razor Creek descent is technical and loose—confidence-testing. Gunnison is a hidden gem: small town, big mountains, zero crowds. The post-race BBQ in the park is low-key awesome—burgers, local beer, live bluegrass. It's not a corporate expo. It's a community celebration. If you can sustain power above 8,000 feet and have low gears for Cochetopa Pass, this is honest mountain suffering without the Leadville circus. Sea-level riders thinking they'll adapt on race day will discover what oxygen debt feels like around mile 40. The terrain isn't technical, but the altitude will expose every fitness gap you've been hiding behind at home.

### Cycle to the Sun — Haleakala
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 36 mi | 10,023 ft | Pa'ia, Maui, Hawaii | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/haleakala-cycle-to-the-sun/

Sea level to 10,023 feet. One climb. One volcano. The House of the Sun. Pure uphill. There is no flat. There is no descent. The road leaves Pa'ia at sea level and climbs continuously for 36 miles through four distinct climate zones to the summit of Haleakala at 10,023 feet. The average gradient is 5%, but sections through Makawao hit 10% and the final summit approach reaches 10-18% in oxygen-depleted air. The landscape transforms completely as you climb: tropical vegetation gives way to eucalyptus, then pine, then barren volcanic rock above the treeline. You will ride above the clouds. You will arrive at the rim of a volcanic crater. Every foot of elevation is earned. Signature challenge: The final 8 miles above the park entrance. Above the cloud line, through barren volcanic landscape, with 70% oxygen and gradients hitting 18%, you grind to the summit crater of Haleakala at 10,023 feet. It is the closest thing to riding on another planet. Cycle to the Sun is not the hardest race in the world, or the most prestigious, or the most competitive. It is, however, one of the most extraordinary cycling experiences on the planet. You start at sea level on a tropical island and ride continuously uphill for 36 miles until you're standing at 10,023 feet on the rim of a volcanic crater, above the clouds, in 40-degree air that was 80 degrees when you started. You traverse every climate zone Hawaii has to offer — tropical, temperate, alpine, volcanic — in a single effort. The name Haleakala means 'House of the Sun,' and when you reach the summit, you understand why. This is not a race you enter to win. This is a ride you do to remind yourself that cycling can take you to places that feel like the edge of the Earth. Yes if you want one of cycling's most extraordinary single-climb experiences. Yes if riding through four climate zones to the summit of a Hawaiian volcano sounds like a lifetime memory. Skip if you need competitive depth or can't justify Hawaii travel costs for a single 36-mile uphill ride. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — 3-5 hour continuous uphill efforts in training; Altitude awareness and preparation for riding above 8,000 ft; Layering strategy tested and rehearsed for 40-degree temperature swing

### La Vaujany Cyclosportive
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 106 mi | 13,000 ft | Vaujany, Isère, France | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/la-vaujany/

Alpine road suffering at its finest: Alpe d'Huez glory awaits the paced climber. Brutal Alpine road sportive featuring iconic paved cols with relentless switchbacks, deep valleys, and high-altitude finishes in the French Oisans massif. Signature challenge: The Alpe d'Huez ascent followed by the Vaujany wall, testing pacing and recovery on Tour de France-proven roads.

### Lauf True Grit Gravel Epic
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 84 mi | 9,000 ft | Santa Clara, Utah | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/lauf-true-grit/

Utah's technical playground where your handling skills matter more than your FTP and the Rollers of Doom separate pretenders from the genuinely prepared. True Grit drops you into the geological chaos where three deserts collide—Mojave, Great Basin, and Colorado Plateau. Eighty percent off-road through sandstone mazes, volcanic remnants, and sandy washes that chew up bikes and spit out undertrained riders. This isn't gravel racing, it's technical mountain biking disguised as a gravel event. Signature challenge: Lines through the rocks that riders practice a dozen times pre-race and still freeze up on race day. The technical sections aren't just difficult—they're bike-destroying, confidence-crushing puzzles that punish hesitation and reward local knowledge you don't have. Show up if you're comfortable dismounting on steep rock faces and practicing technical lines a dozen times before race day. The 9,000 feet of climbing rides like a century because of the constant technical demands—your bike handling needs to be sharp when you're deep into hour six. Skip this if you prefer smooth gravel roads or think a dropper post is overkill for gravel racing.

### Le Grand du Nord
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 110 mi | 5,000 ft | Grand Marais, Minnesota | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/le-grand-du-nord/

The most civilized way to suffer through 110 miles of Minnesota Northwoods while pretending Lake Superior views make your legs hurt less. Rolling terrain through Minnesota Northwoods. 5,000 ft over 110 miles = constant ups and downs, no sustained climbs. Mix of smooth gravel forest roads (70%), paved sections (20%), and technical doubletrack (10%). Smoother than mountain gravel but never flat. Signature challenge: Steep, scenic climb with Lake Superior views. Pack may still be together here—surges happen. Le Grand du Nord is Minnesota's entry into the UCI Gravel World Series—2,000+ riders racing through the stunning North Shore of Lake Superior at peak spring green. The course rolls through dense Northwoods, climbs for Lake Superior overlooks, and finishes in the tourist town of Grand Marais. It's not the hardest course (5,000 ft over 110 miles = rolling, not mountainous), but the field is international and fast. The scenery is world-class: dense Minnesota woods, Lake Superior vistas, and the iconic Gunflint Trail. Grand Marais is the perfect race town—hotels, restaurants, breweries, and post-race tourism. The large field means pack racing early and potential bottlenecks, but it thins out. This is a big-event, well-organized UCI race with all the amenities. You need the fitness for 110 miles and 5,000 feet—that's six hours minimum of rolling punishment through forest roads. The course isn't technically brutal, but it's relentlessly rolling, and those doubletrack sections at mile 80 will test your focus when you're cooked. If you can't handle pack racing with 2,000 riders or the possibility of 45-degree rain, stay home.

### L'Etape Ireland
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 93 mi | 6,200 ft | Carrick-on-Suir, County Tipperary, Ireland | September | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-ireland/

TdF magic on Irish mountains: challenging, scenic, accessible.

### L'Etape Taiwan by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 64.6 mi | 4,921 ft | Sun Moon Lake, Nantou County, Taiwan | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-taiwan/

TdF flair conquers Taiwan's lake paradise Starts fast downhill from Xiangshan into Puli flats, then unleashes KOM beast on Road 147. Winding lake returns via Road 21 grind steady pain amid emerald bliss. Pro closed roads amplify Tour intensity. Signature challenge: The Road 147 KOM: 7km of escalating pain peaking at 12%, freshly paved for max suffering. Tests resolve amid rice fields—true TdF suffering in Taiwan. L'Etape Taiwan transplants Tour de France magic to Sun Moon Lake's stunning shores, blending 1,500m Taiwanese climbs with pro spectacle. Fresh pavement and global fields make it a must for adventure seekers tired of Alps reruns. Humidity tests grit, but emerald vistas and prestige jersey reward every watt. Emerging star that's already essential. Yes if craving prestige jersey and Asian vibes with manageable suffering. Skip if mega-elevation junkie or hate humidity. Non-negotiables: 12+ hours endurance rides with 2,000ft climbs; Descending confidence on 4% wet roads

### L'Etape Turkey by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 66.4 mi | 5,200 ft | Beykoz, Istanbul, Turkey | September | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-turkey/

Tour prestige meets Turkish splendor in a 106km stunner. Beykoz loop unleashes Istanbul's wild side—paved climbs snake through forests, villages, and pavilions. Traffic-free bliss lets you smash king-of-mountain segments amid Polonezkoy charm. 106km blends endurance grind with touristic euphoria, Tour-style without the jet lag. Signature challenge: Beykoz's cumulative climbs rack 1600m+ over 66 miles, blending relentless gradients with Istanbul panoramas. No single monster but endless testing forges Tour grit. Traffic-free freedom amplifies the hurt. L'Etape Turkey blasts onto the scene fusing Bosphorus beauty with Tour de France pedigree—106km of paved punishment through Beykoz's hidden gems. Traffic-free pro treatment elevates amateurs to heroes amid 3K global riders. Prestige punches way above untested climbs; Istanbul's allure seals the deal for adventure-starved roadies. Minor unknowns like vert pale against the hype. Yes if craving exotic road prestige sans Alps agony. Skip if chasing 4K+ vert or gravel grit. Non-negotiables: 50mi weeks with 5Kft climbing; Two 80km sim rides

### London Edinburgh London
