The only US path to UCI Gravel Worlds—67 miles of Ozark smashfest. A concentrated smashfest through Ozark gravel. Tree canopy covers much of the course, providing shade but limiting descent visibility. The gravel is chunky and sharp—not smooth hardpack. Over a dozen climbs in 67 miles means you're either going up or bombing down. No recovery. Signature challenge: The relentless rollers on chunky sharp gravel—death by a thousand cuts with technical descents mixed in. Highlands Gravel Classic is the only US path to UCI Gravel World Championships—that alone makes it significant. The race itself is a 67-mile smashfest through chunky Ozark gravel with constant climbs and technical descents. It's short enough to race hard and long enough to hurt. For anyone chasing UCI qualification, this is mandatory. Yes if you're chasing UCI qualification. Yes if you want a short, intense race on technical terrain. Skip if you want ultra-endurance or smooth gravel.

### Granfondo Sestriere — Colle delle Finestre
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 75 mi | 10,400 ft | Cesana Torinese, Piedmont, Italy | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/marmotte-granfondo-sestriere/

The only granfondo in the world with Colle delle Finestre's legendary unpaved summit. Giro d'Italia dirt at 2,178 meters. A focused Alpine assault centered on one defining climb. The route through the Susa and Chisone Valleys is scenic and demanding, but everyone is here for the same reason: Colle delle Finestre. The course builds through steady valley climbs and fast descents before delivering you to the base of the Finestre's 18.5 km ascent. The first 10 km on tarmac are relentless (9.2% average), but the real event begins when the road surface changes. The final 7.8 km on dirt — switchbacking through larch forests with gradients reaching 14% — is the granfondo's entire reason for existence. The descent and return climb to Sestriere at 2,035m ensures the legs never fully recover. Signature challenge: The 7.8 km of unpaved road on Colle delle Finestre. The transition from tarmac to dirt at approximately 1,700m altitude — on gradients that reach 14%, at oxygen levels measurably reduced by altitude — is the single most distinctive feature of any road granfondo in the world. You can train for the climbing. You cannot fully prepare for the psychological impact of losing your road surface when the gradient kicks hardest. The Granfondo Sestriere exists for one reason: Colle delle Finestre. And that one reason is enough. This is the only organized cycling event in the world that sends you up the 7.8 km of unpaved road that has produced some of the most dramatic Giro d'Italia stages in modern history. The event itself is young and still finding its identity after splitting from the Marmotte brand, but the mountain does not care about branding. When the tarmac ends and the gravel begins at 1,700m, you are riding the exact surface that broke champions and made legends. Everything else — the Susa Valley, the Olympic infrastructure, the finish at Sestriere — is context for that singular experience. Yes if you want to ride cycling's most famous unpaved climb in an organized event. Yes if you value Giro d'Italia heritage and want to feel what the professionals felt on that dirt. Yes if you want a high-value Italian Alpine experience without the logistics of deep-Alpine events. Skip if you need a large, prestigious international granfondo. Skip if you want a 150+ km all-day epic — at 103 km, this is more focused than sprawling. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing endurance — 2-3 hour continuous climbing efforts in training; Road bike setup tested on rough surfaces — tire choice and pressure dialed; Full dress rehearsal — 3+ hours of climbing with nutrition and descending strategy

### Rock Cobbler
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 100 mi | 5,000 ft | Bakersfield, California | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rock-cobbler/

The gravel scene's greatest shitshow — equal parts bike race, agricultural obstacle course, and fever dream cooked up by someone who thinks cyclocross isn't weird enough. 100 miles of Central Valley gravel and dirt. 5,000 ft elevation. 60% gravel, 30% dirt, 10% pavement. Chunky rocky sections. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: February weather roulette. Chunky rocks. Early season fitness test. Rock Cobbler is California's early season gravel classic—100 miles of Central Valley rocks, dirt, and Havilah climb. Founded by Stephen Fry in 2012. 600+ riders. February timing means weather roulette: 2023 was rain/mud (25%+ DNF), 2024 was perfect. Bakersfield is affordable ($80-120 lodging). Chunky rocky sections reward technical skills. Peter Stetina & Lauren De Crescenzo won 2024. If you're California-based and want early season test, it's great value ($80-$120). If you're flying in, weather risk is real. If you need your races predictable and your routes announced more than a week out, keep scrolling. This is for riders who want their fitness tested by 30% run-ups, cattle dodging, and whatever Sam Ames dreamed up the night before. Your FTP matters less than your ability to laugh while hike-a-biking through a ball pit. Show up undertrained and the scree fields will humble you in ways Strava can't measure.

### The Gralloch
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 124 mi | 11,483 ft | Scottish Highlands, Scotland | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-gralloch/

Scotland's gravel crown jewel serves up 1,700 meters of climbing through Galloway's forests with a side of Highland hospitality. 200 km, 3,500 m. 75% gravel, 25% singletrack/dirt. Scottish Highlands—remote technical terrain. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 200 km ultra + Scottish weather (40% rain, cold) + remote Highlands. 25-35% DNF. The Gralloch is Scottish Highlands ultra—200 km, 3,500 m elevation. Founded ~2017. 75% gravel, 25% singletrack/dirt. Remote Highlands terrain, technical sections. 200-300 riders. September autumn conditions (8-18°C, 40% rain). 25-35% DNF. Ultra distance (12-18 hours). Self-sufficient required. If you want Scottish ultra brutality with remote Highlands scenery, this is it. If you want manageable distance or good weather, skip it. Brutal but unique. You need solid climbing legs and comfort on technical gravel—that opening 11km ascent eliminates pretenders fast. The 4:15 PM cutoff at 67km isn't generous, so show up fit or get shuttled to the shortened route. Perfect for riders who want UCI World Series prestige without the chaos of American mega-events.

### Tour de Big Bear
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 100 mi | 8,500 ft | Big Bear Lake, California, USA | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-de-big-bear/

100 miles at 7,000 feet with legendary aid stations that serve ribs, have DJs, and make you forget you are dying on a bike. A deceptive course that lulls you with rolling lakeside roads for 50 miles before delivering the altitude hammer in the second half. The Onyx Summit climb at 8,443 feet is the crown jewel, and the Cedar Falls descent-and-return is where tired legs at altitude learn about humility. Signature challenge: Onyx Summit at 8,443 feet. The highest paved road in Southern California, reached after 50+ miles of riding at altitude. The KOM/QOM competition makes the climb honest. Then you descend 2,000 feet to Cedar Falls and must climb back up. The Tour de Big Bear is what happens when a Southern California cycling community builds their dream ride: 100 miles at 7,000 feet with 8,500 feet of climbing, legendary aid stations that serve BBQ ribs and have DJ booths, and a mountain lake setting that delivers 55-65F start temperatures while the rest of SoCal melts in August.

The climbing is real. Onyx Summit at 8,443 feet is the highest paved road in Southern California, and the KOM competition up its slopes draws genuine efforts. The profile is back-loaded and punishing: rolling lakeside roads lull you for 50 miles, then the Onyx push and Cedar Falls loop deliver the hurt. The 12:30 PM cutoff at Blue Sky Meadows is there for a reason -- undertrained riders bonk at altitude.

But the aid stations are what make this event legendary. Full buffets, DJs, couches, ribs, live music. The volunteers are locals who have been doing this for 15 years and take genuine pride in their hospitality. Named the #1 Gran Fondo in North America by Gran Fondo Guide in 2023. If you are a SoCal cyclist, this is your annual pilgrimage. Yes if you want the definitive SoCal mountain cycling experience with altitude, climbing, and aid stations that redefine hospitality. Skip if you cannot handle 7,000+ foot altitude, need a sanctioned race, or live far from California. Non-negotiables: Altitude acclimatization strategy; Century-distance climbing fitness; Heat management for Cedar Falls descent

### BWR Arizona
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 104 mi | 6,600 ft | Cave Creek / McDowell Region, Arizona | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-arizona/

Technical desert suffering through sand, rock, and singletrack—a true BWR experience in Sonoran landscapes. A desert gravel experience that emphasizes technical unroad over pure climbing. Sand washes, rocky singletrack, and punchy desert climbs through Sonoran landscapes. The BWR formula adapted to high-desert terrain. Signature challenge: The combination of sand, rock, and technical singletrack in desert conditions—'more unroad' means the terrain difficulty compensates for lower elevation gain. BWR Arizona delivers the BWR suffering formula in a desert package—technical singletrack, sand, rocks, and punchy climbs through Sonoran landscapes. It's less climby than California but compensates with unroad technical demands. Not an major series race, but attracts that caliber of competition. The 'lowest vert BWR' is still a beast. Yes if you want technical gravel racing with genuine unroad terrain. Yes if you're chasing BWR cred without major series pressure. Skip if you expect smooth gravel roads or hate sand and rocks.

### Cyclassics Hamburg
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 96 mi | 1,150 ft | Hamburg, Germany | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cyclassics-hamburg/

Free entry. 22,000 riders. Closed roads through Germany's second city. The Waseberg at 16%. Europe's biggest cycling party — not its hardest ride. Yes if free registration and world-class ASO organization sound too good to pass up. Yes if a cycling festival weekend in Hamburg appeals. Skip if you need climbing, technical terrain, or a genuine physical test — this is flat and short. Skip if you want adventure, wilderness, or remoteness — this is a city sportive. Non-negotiables: Group riding confidence — comfortable at 30-35+ km/h in a peloton of 100+ riders; Short steep climb preparation — 1-3 minute all-out efforts after extended flat riding; Wind riding experience — ability to echelon and draft in crosswinds on flat, exposed roads

### Sea Otter Fuego XL
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 70 mi | 7,800 ft | Monterey, California | April | mtb
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/fuego-mtb/

Sea Otter's sandbox climbs and corporate chaos create a perfectly mediocre gravel experience wrapped in mountain bike tradition. Fort Ord's sandy climbs and loose-over-hard descents turn your bike into a wrestling match. Two laps of relentless XC terrain where the sandstone gets looser and the ruts get deeper as 2,000 riders churn the course into a minefield of blown lines and shattered legs. Signature challenge: The sandy climbs don't negotiate with your fitness or technique. Fort Ord's sandstone terrain punishes every inefficient pedal stroke and rewards only those who've learned to dance with loose dirt. By lap two, the course has been churned into a minefield where clean lines disappear and mechanical sympathy becomes survival. If you can hold 250+ watts for three hours and enjoy fighting sandy ruts while dodging expo traffic, this works. The course isn't technical but those loose-over-hard descents will humble cocky roadies, and April weather swings from 40 to 90 degrees without warning. Skip it if you want pure gravel culture – this is MTB pageantry with drop bars allowed.

### Gran Fondo Hincapie
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 83 mi | 8,100 ft | Travelers Rest, South Carolina | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-hincapie/

George Hincapie's Tour de France training roads. Skyuka Mountain at 8.7%. The Southeast's premier gran fondo with 15 years of pedigree. George Hincapie trained on these roads for 17 Tours de France, and the Gran route makes you understand why. The 83-mile course from Hotel Domestique punches north into the Blue Ridge foothills, crossing into North Carolina's Polk County for the signature climbs before looping back to the Travelers Rest finish. Three timed segments — Skyuka Mountain, Green River Cove, and the final climb to the finish — structure the suffering around sustained, gradient-heavy climbing. Between the major climbs, rolling Blue Ridge countryside and Lake Road's technical turns keep the legs honest. The autumn foliage through dense forest canopy creates postcard scenery that's easy to miss when you're redlined on Skyuka's 8.7% gradient. This is a road event on paved surfaces throughout, but the mountainous character, the altitude variation from 1,100 ft to nearly 3,000 ft, and the relentless vertical accumulation make it one of the hardest gran fondos in the Eastern United States. Signature challenge: Skyuka Mountain. Four miles at 8.7% average gradient, 1,814 feet of vertical gain, a summit at 2,967 feet, and a technical descent so steep that carbon clincher rim brakes are banned from the course. This is where the Gran Fondo Hincapie earns the right to carry a Tour de France veteran's name. George Hincapie spent 17 years as professional cycling's most trusted domestique — the rider who sacrificed personal glory so others could stand on the Tour de France podium. His retirement project is the opposite philosophy: an event designed entirely for your experience. Gran Fondo Hincapie routes through the Blue Ridge foothill roads where Hincapie actually trained for July in France, summiting Skyuka Mountain at 8.7% and navigating Green River Cove's switchbacks — the same climbs that built the engine behind nine Tour de France champions. The 83-mile Gran with 8,100 feet of climbing is legitimate suffering, not a branded fun ride. But the Hotel Domestique base, the Michelin-starred chef dinner, the post-ride festival, and the parade of cycling legends create an experience layer that most gran fondos can only aspire to. The 2024 Hurricane Helene pivot — when the event raised $750,000 for the devastated Polk County communities that had hosted the fondo for 13 years — showed that this isn't corporate cycling tourism. It's an event with a soul. Yes if riding a Tour de France domestique's personal training roads alongside cycling legends appeals to you. Yes if you want a legitimate 83-mile, 8,100-foot climbing challenge wrapped in a luxury cycling weekend. Skip if you need competitive racing depth — this is participatory, not a race. Skip if premium pricing for a branded experience isn't your thing. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing fitness — repeated 15-30 minute threshold efforts on 6-9% gradients; 80+ mile endurance base with 6,000+ feet of climbing in training; Descent confidence — practice technical switchback descents at speed

### Gran Fondo Marco Pantani
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 90 mi | 7,054 ft | Cesenatico, Emilia-Romagna, Italy | September | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-marco-pantani/

Il Pirata's training roads. His family's event. Cesenatico to the Romagnol hills and back. Since 2005. Three routes of increasing ambition, all starting from the Marco Pantani monument on the Cesenatico waterfront and heading inland through the Romagnol hills. The terrain transitions from flat Adriatic coastal roads to rolling countryside to the punchy, steep-gradient hills that characterize the pre-Apennine foothills. The Lungo route ventures into the Marche border country, adding the Botticella and additional climbing to the Ciola and Montevecchio that feature on all routes. The rhythm is distinctly Romagnol — short, sharp climbs separated by rolling valleys with small villages, vineyards, and stone walls. These are not long Alpine grinds; they are the repeated punchy efforts that Pantani used to build explosive power before translating it to the high mountains. Signature challenge: The cumulative climbing across the Ciola, Botticella, and Montevecchio is honest rather than heroic — 2,150m on the Lungo route. The real challenge is emotional. These are the roads where Marco Pantani trained. The gradients he ground out on winter mornings. The hills he repeated until his legs could dance on the pedals at 90 rpm uphill. Riding them with the knowledge of what he became — and how it ended — adds a dimension that no elevation profile can capture. The Gran Fondo Marco Pantani is not the hardest ride in Italy, not the most scenic, and not the most famous. It is the most personal. Organized by the Pantani family, starting from the bronze statue of Il Pirata on the Cesenatico waterfront, following the roads he rode as a teenager and as a Tour de France champion, passing the monument at Montevecchio where his spirit lives in the hills. Marco Pantani — born in Cesenatico 1970, dead in Rimini 2004, the last man to win the Giro and the Tour in the same year — built his legendary climbing legs on these Romagnol hills before he ever touched the Galibier or the Mortirolo. This event lets you ride in his ghost. The climbs are honest, the countryside is charming, and the emotional weight is unlike anything else in amateur cycling. Yes if Pantani's story moves you and you want to ride the roads where he built his legendary climbing engine. Yes if September in Italy with warm weather and honest Romagnol hills sounds like your kind of weekend. Skip if you want pure athletic challenge — Nove Colli in the same town offers more distance and climbing. Skip if you have no connection to Pantani's legacy — without that context, this is a pleasant but mid-tier Italian sportive. Non-negotiables: Hill repeat training — sustained efforts on 6-8% gradients for 15-25 minutes; Multi-climb endurance — 4+ distinct climbs in a single training ride; 145 km distance capability with climbing — full dress rehearsal

### Gran Fondo Mont-Tremblant
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 93 mi | — | Mont-Tremblant, Quebec, Canada | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-mont-tremblant/

Scenic, well-organized road sportive in Laurentians ideal for season-opening endurance challenge.

### Gran Fondo Zadar
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 62 mi | 2,800 ft | Zadar, Croatia | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-zadar/

Scenic Adriatic sportive with Velebit climbs and resort perks.

### Granfondo Belgium
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 85.7 mi | 6,890 ft | Les Lacs de l'Eau d'Heure, Hainaut, Belgium | March | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-belgium/

Car-free Ardennes qualifier punches way above rookie weight. Ardennes microcosm: 33km opener splits the pack on 3km@3% (8%max) before 4x24km laps south to Couvin. Lakeside calm yields to valley crushers. Paved, rolling brutality tests pacing over raw distance. Signature challenge: Fourfold Petit Poggio laps forge unbreakable engines amid repeat suffering. Cumulative 2100m demands fueling mastery. Car-free flow lets pain purify. Granfondo Belgium 2.0 torches its turbulent debut with car-free roads and selective savagery around Eau d'Heure's emerald basins. 138km of Ardennes pain qualifies elites while capping chaos at 2k riders. UCI prestige meets Belgian grit—spring sharpeners, this is your qualifier ticket. Laps grind souls but scenery soothes; post-2025 glow makes it unmissable. Yes if chasing UCI worlds quals with Ardennes appetite and rainproof grit. Skip if spring slop or lap monotony kills your vibe—wait for field maturity. Non-negotiables: 12+ hours threshold power at 4w/kg; Full wet-weather kit + mudguards

### Gravel and Tar Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 99 mi | 6,562 ft | Manawatū-Whanganui, New Zealand | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-and-tar-classic/

New Zealand's most beautiful way to discover that punctures and time cuts don't negotiate. 160 km, 2,000 m. 70% gravel, 30% paved. New Zealand countryside—rolling hills, farmland. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: 160 km long distance + 2,000 m elevation. New Zealand countryside. Gravel and Tar Classic is New Zealand countryside—160 km, 2,000 m elevation. Founded ~2018. 70% gravel, 30% paved. New Zealand countryside—rolling hills, farmland. 300-500 riders. Manawatū-Whanganui, NZ. February summer conditions (18-28°C). Affordable entry (NZ$150-$200 / $90-$120). If you want New Zealand countryside with long distance and affordable entry, this is it. If you need short distance or local/domestic, skip it. 160 km is long. International destination race. If you can handle 99 miles where the gravel sectors exist specifically to fracture the field and leave riders scattered across the Manawatū countryside, this is your kind of suffering. The 25% gravel ratio sounds friendly until you're dealing with punctures while chasing time cuts that historically claim a significant chunk of the field. Show up undertrained and you'll join the long list of DNFs who underestimated what 'toughest one-day race in Oceania' actually means.

### Gravel Roubaix
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 100 mi | 8,000 ft | Palmerton, Pennsylvania | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-roubaix/

A proper gravel beatdown disguised as a backyard Pennsylvania sufferfest. Barry-Roubaix throws everything at you except actual cobblestones. Eighty percent gravel roads with pavement breaks, sand traps, mud wallows, and one brutal mile of rocky two-track that separates pretenders from finishers. The terrain rolls constantly through Michigan farmland, never letting you settle into a rhythm. Signature challenge: Sager Road is the course's true character test—a narrow, unmaintained seasonal road that can be sandy, rocky, or muddy depending on conditions. It's where separation occurs in the front of the race because fitness alone won't save you when your bike handling skills get exposed on technical terrain at race pace. Show up ready for 8,000 feet of climbing and terrain that switches moods faster than spring weather. The Three Sisters will expose weak legs early, and Sager Road's sandy chaos separates pretenders from contenders. You need solid bike handling skills and the fitness to climb repeatedly—this isn't a gentle introduction to gravel racing.

### Grinduro Canada
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 67 mi | 5,900 ft | Saint-Urbain-de-Charlevoix, Quebec, Canada | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro-canada/

A mountain bike race disguised as gravel, where meteorite-shaped terrain separates the prepared from the delusional. Grinduro Canada drops you inside a 450-million-year-old meteorite crater where Mother Nature has been plotting your demise since the Ordovician period. Four timed segments demand everything from singletrack handling to sand survival, connected by 'neutral' sections that aren't neutral at all. This isn't gravel racing—it's enduro with a gravel entry fee. Signature challenge: The course pretends to be gravel but demands full mountain bike skills across four timed segments that expose every weakness in your technical repertoire. Ancient meteorite impacts created terrain that laughs at road bike geometry and narrow tires—show up on the wrong equipment and the crater will humble you publicly. If you panic on technical singletrack or think 25mm tires are acceptable, stay home. This demands MTB skills, fat rubber, and comfort with getting your ego adjusted by Quebec sand pits. The festival vibes and excellent food soften the blow, but won't save you from walking the steep, loose climbs that eat narrow tires alive.

### HalvVättern

Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/halvvattern/

Iconic half-distance sportive around southern Lake Vättern, blending challenge, scenery, and community.

### Jämtlandspilen

Ultimate Swedish road ultra for endurance junkies craving value-packed suffering. A demanding road ultra looping through Jämtland's forested hills and lake-dotted plains around Östersund, blending steady climbs with fast rollers on smooth tarmac. Signature challenge: The 'Jämtland Wall' - a 2-mile grind at 7% average near the 100-mile mark, often deciding the race in crosswinds.

### Les Trois Ballons

### Pan-Mass Challenge (PMC)
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 177 mi | 6,800 ft | Worcester to Provincetown, Massachusetts, USA (multiple routes across Massachusetts) | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/pan-mass-challenge/

6,500 riders. 177 miles. $1.125 billion raised. The bike ride that fights cancer. A two-day crossing of Massachusetts on paved roads, from the rolling hills of central Mass to the windswept tip of Cape Cod. Day 1 is the harder day — 100 miles from Worcester featuring genuine New England climbing in the first 50 miles (long, rolling ascents and fast descents), gradually flattening as the route approaches the Cape Cod Canal and the finish at Mass Maritime Academy in Bourne. The overnight at MMA is a PMC institution — thousands of riders sleeping in dorms, tents, and ship quarters, fueling for Day 2. Sunday's 77 miles from Bourne to Provincetown are rolling-to-flat Cape Cod terrain with ocean breezes, cranberry bogs, and the charming towns of the outer Cape, punctuated by short steep climbs in Truro before the emotional finish at the Provincetown Monument. Signature challenge: The emotional weight. The PMC's signature challenge is not a climb or a headwind — it is riding 177 miles while carrying the names and stories of people fighting cancer. The physical effort is real. The emotional effort is greater. The Pan-Mass Challenge is not a bike ride. It is a $78-million-a-year cancer research funding machine that uses bicycles as its engine. Since 1980, when Billy Starr convinced 36 riders to cycle from Springfield to Provincetown, the PMC has raised $1.125 billion for Dana-Farber Cancer Institute — 100% of every rider-raised dollar going directly to research, treatment, and patient care. Today, 6,500 riders cross Massachusetts on routes from 25 to 177 miles, supported by 3,500 volunteers and an organization refined over 46 years. The cycling is honest New England terrain — rolling hills, coastal roads, and a two-day format that demands real fitness. But the experience transcends sport. You ride past signs that say 'Thank you — my daughter is alive because of Dana-Farber.' You pedal alongside survivors wearing jerseys with their diagnosis dates. You finish in Provincetown knowing that your effort — every dollar you raised, every mile you rode — went directly to the fight. The PMC does not ask you to race. It asks you to matter. You do not race the PMC — you ride it with purpose. Yes if you are ready to commit to raising $2,000-$6,000+ for cancer research and want the most emotionally powerful cycling experience in America. Yes if you can handle 100+ miles of rolling New England roads in August heat. Skip if you need competitive cycling, dramatic terrain, or cannot commit to the fundraising requirement. Non-negotiables: Back-to-back long ride capability — 80+ miles Saturday, 60+ miles Sunday; Heat and humidity training — extended rides in warm conditions; Fundraising commitment met and confirmed

### Sovev Jerusalem
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 31 mi | 4,921 ft | Jerusalem, Israel | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/sovev-jerusalem/

Jerusalem's iconic mass cycling celebration blending athletic challenge with historic wonders.

### Dekom System Sudety Tour
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 93 mi | 8,200 ft | Broumov, Czechia | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/sudety-tour/

A technically demanding, culturally rich cross-border gran fondo with authentic climbing challenges, hampered by limited logistical infrastructure and uncertain field depth. A challenging, undulating cross-border gran fondo through the Sudetes mountains. The route alternates between forest climbs, cobblestone sections, and rolling terrain, with constant elevation changes throughout. The grandfondo features two major climbs and incorporates both Czechia and Poland, creating a unique multinational cycling experience. Signature challenge: The combination of the 10 km Polish forest ascent and the brutal 1 km cobblestone climb at Vysoka Srbska, separated by rolling terrain that prevents full recovery. The mediofondo shortcuts the eastern loops but retains both signature climbs. Non-negotiables: Climbing fitness: 2,086 m elevation in 152 km demands strong sustained climbing ability; Technical bike handling: Cobblestone sections and forest roads require confident descending and technical control; Self-sufficiency: Remote location and limited logistical infrastructure require independent problem-solving

### Tour de l'Île de Montréal
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 31 mi | 500 ft | Montreal, Quebec, Canada | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-de-lile-de-montreal/

25,000 riders circle the island of Montreal on car-free streets. A former Guinness World Record holder, now in its 41st year. You do not race the Tour de l'Île — you ride it. Yes if you want to experience Montreal from a bicycle on car-free streets with 25,000 other cyclists. Yes if you want a family-friendly, affordable, superbly organized mass-participation event. Yes if you want to visit one of the world's great cycling cities during its most beautiful season. Skip if you need athletic challenge, competitive racing, elevation, or technical terrain. Non-negotiables: Comfort riding in dense groups — practice holding a line in traffic; Rain gear and layering strategy — check the forecast; Hydration and nutrition plan — do not rely solely on rest stops

### Tour de Yorkshire Sportive
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 87 mi | 6,890 ft | Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-de-yorkshire-sportive/

Tour de France heritage. Yorkshire climbs. Roadside passion. On hiatus since 2019 — but if it returns, ride it. The Tour de Yorkshire Sportive changed its route each year to mirror the professional race's final stage, giving riders a rotating tour of Yorkshire's finest cycling terrain. The consistent element was the terrain character: short, steep, punishing climbs with exposed moorland between them, narrow English lanes, and descents that demanded respect in wet conditions. Buttertubs (6 km at 6% with 20% ramps), Grinton Moor, Cote de Harwood Dale, and similar Yorkshire classics rotated through. The route always included the final 50 km and finish line of the pro race, and riders could watch the professional peloton cross the same line hours later. Signature challenge: Buttertubs. Six kilometers at 6% average with pitches hitting 20%. Exposed, relentless, and iconic. The quintessential Yorkshire climb — short enough to be over quickly, steep enough to make you wish it was over sooner. The Tour de Yorkshire Sportive was born from the most electrifying moment in British cycling history: the 2014 Tour de France Grand Depart. When the Tour came to Yorkshire, 2.6 million people lined the roads. A cycling culture was ignited. The professional Tour de Yorkshire launched in 2015, and the sportive gave amateurs the chance to ride the same roads, hours before the pros.

The format was simple and powerful: ride the route of the final stage — including its iconic Yorkshire climbs — then watch the pro race from the finish line. The routes varied each year: Leeds, Scarborough, Sheffield, each bringing different Yorkshire terrain. Buttertubs, Grinton Moor, Cote de Harwood Dale — the classic climbs rotated through, keeping each edition fresh.

The event is currently on hiatus. The professional Tour de Yorkshire was cancelled from 2020 onwards (COVID, then financial difficulties with Welcome to Yorkshire), and the sportive has not returned. If it does, the combination of Tour de France heritage, Yorkshire's passionate cycling community, and the county's beautiful-brutal terrain makes it one of the UK's strongest sportive propositions. Yes if the event returns and you want to ride iconic Yorkshire climbs with the Tour de France heritage and passionate roadside support. Yes if English cycling culture and the Dales landscape appeal to you. Skip if the event remains on hiatus. Skip if you need guaranteed good weather — Yorkshire in late April is a gamble. Non-negotiables: Short, steep hill repeats — 5-10 minute efforts at 10-15% gradient, minimum 8 repetitions; Wet weather riding practice — descending on wet English roads with confidence; Full kit for all conditions — rain jacket, arm warmers, gilet, overshoes packed and accessible

### Tour Down Under Community Ride
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 93 mi | 4,921 ft | Adelaide, South Australia | January | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-down-under-community-ride/

Fun, supported ride on pro WorldTour roads with Willunga Hill climax amid Adelaide's cycling festival.

### Velothon Berlin
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 99 mi | 2,000 ft | Berlin, Germany | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/velothon-berlin/

Berlin's fastest, flashiest road race - pure speed through history's epicenter. Lightning-fast urban circuit showcasing Berlin's landmarks on completely closed roads, prioritizing pure speed and spectator energy over climbing challenges. Signature challenge: Sustaining 45+ kph average speeds for 99 miles on flat roads past Reichstag, East Side Gallery, and Victory Column with 13,000 competitors.

### Alpe d'HuZes
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 51 mi | 21,089 ft | Alpe d'Huez, Isere, France | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/alpe-dhuzes/

Twenty-one hairpins. Six ascents. EUR 100 million for cancer research. Cycling's most profound charity event. One climb. Repeated. The 21 hairpins of Alpe d'Huez — 13.8 km at 7.9% average, with sections exceeding 11% — climbed up to six times in a single day. The route is simple to describe and punishing to execute. Each ascent follows the same road: the steep lower hairpins through the forest, the more open middle section, and the final push into the resort village at 1,860m. Each descent follows the same road back to Bourg d'Oisans. The physical experience changes with each repetition — ascent one is exhilarating, ascent three is grim, ascent six is transcendent. The emotional experience depends entirely on why you're climbing. Signature challenge: Repetition. One ascent of Alpe d'Huez is a great cycling experience. Six ascents transform it into something else entirely — a test of physical endurance, mental fortitude, and the power of purpose. The mountain doesn't change, but you do. Alpe d'HuZes is not a cycling event that happens to raise money for charity. It is a charity event that happens to use cycling's most legendary mountain as its vehicle. The distinction matters. When 5,000 riders climb Alpe d'Huez up to six times in a single day — many wearing jerseys bearing the names of people they've lost to cancer — the physical achievement becomes secondary to the emotional one. The 21 hairpins are still the 21 hairpins, the gradients are still punishing, and six ascents produce more vertical than almost any single-day cycling event on Earth. But what elevates Alpe d'HuZes beyond any sportive or gran fondo is the why. EUR 100 million raised. The largest sporting charity event in the Netherlands. This is cycling at its most human. Yes if you want to climb cycling's most famous mountain for a cause that transcends sport. Yes if the Dutch charity community and the emotional atmosphere appeal to you as much as the physical challenge. Skip if you want a traditional sportive with varied terrain and competitive structure. Skip if the EUR 1,500 fundraising minimum is a barrier. Non-negotiables: Repeated climbing sessions — 3-4 hours of sustained climbing with short recovery between efforts; Fundraising target met (EUR 1,500 minimum); Full dress rehearsal — 4+ hours of repeated climbing with nutrition and pacing strategy

### Belgrade Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 75 mi | 5,906 ft | Belgrade, Serbia | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/belgrade-gran-fondo/

Belgrade's premier gran fondo blending city vibes, mountain challenge, and Balkan hospitality.

### Bend Dirt Fest
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 50 mi | 3,000 ft | Bend, Oregon | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bend-dirt-fest/

A solid debut that prioritizes good vibes over gravel glory, perfect for testing Oregon's dirt without the Oregon trail suffering. High desert mix: gravel roads, doubletrack, singletrack. Multi-discipline festival course. 50 miles, ~3,000 ft. Oregon volcanic landscape. Signature challenge: High desert heat and sun exposure. Singletrack technical sections. Bend Dirt Fest is the Oregon Trail Cycling Series finale and a multi-discipline festival—gravel, MTB, trail run, all in one weekend. This isn't a pure gravel race; it's a celebration of Bend's outdoor culture. The course mixes high desert gravel roads, doubletrack, and singletrack. Field is small (~500 across all events), vibe is community-focused. If you're looking for a competitive gravel race, look elsewhere. If you want a fun weekend in Bend with friends, beer, and dirt, this is it. This works if you want a legitimate 50-mile dirt challenge without the circus atmosphere of bigger events. The 3,000 feet of climbing won't break anyone, but it'll remind you why base fitness matters. Skip it if you need competition depth or proven course logistics—first-year events always come with question marks.

### Deccan Cliffhanger
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 75 mi | — | Pune to Mahabaleshwar, Maharashtra, India | November | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/deccan-cliffhanger/

Iconic Indian sportive with brutal Ghats climbs and stunning plateau-to-hill views.

### Engadin Radmarathon
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 137 mi | 13,123 ft | Zernez, Engadin Valley, Graubunden, Switzerland | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/engadin-radmarathon/

Three days of Swiss Alpine precision. Five passes above 2,000m. Glaciers, national parks, and UNESCO heritage — through the Engadin, where the roads are perfect and the scenery is better. A three-act Alpine drama staged across the Engadin Valley. The Friday prologue is a sharp appetizer: 6.7 km straight up toward the Ofenpass through the Swiss National Park, legs burning from the first pedal stroke. Saturday's La Cuorta is the international chapter: through a closed tunnel into the duty-free Italian enclave of Livigno, over the Forcola di Livigno and the Bernina Pass in rapid succession — with the glaciers of Piz Bernina and Piz Palu gleaming above the descent to Pontresina. Sunday's La Svizra is the masterpiece: the Fluela Pass at 2,383m rising from Susch in 13 km of sustained climbing, a fast descent into Davos, the long Landwasser Valley approach to the Albula Pass alongside the UNESCO World Heritage Rhaetian Railway, and the final descent back into the Engadin to Zernez. The cumulative effect of three days, five passes, and 221 km at altitude is a slow-building immersion in some of the finest cycling terrain in the Alps. Signature challenge: The Albula Pass on Sunday. After two days of Alpine climbing — five passes already in the legs — the 26 km approach from the Landwasser Valley alongside the UNESCO World Heritage Rhaetian Railway is the event's emotional and physical crescendo. The climb is not the steepest, not the highest, not the most dramatic. It is the most relentless — and it comes when you have the least to give. The Engadin Radmarathon is what happens when Swiss organizational culture meets some of the most spectacular cycling terrain in the Alps. Three days, five passes above 2,000 meters, 221 km of pristine tarmac through the Engadin Valley — crossing into Italy, riding through the Swiss National Park, climbing alongside a UNESCO World Heritage railway, and staring at the Bernina glacier massif from the saddle. It is not the hardest Alpine sportive (the Otztaler and La Marmotte are harder). It is not the most famous (the Maratona and L'Etape are better known). What it is, consistently, is one of the most beautiful — and one of the best organized. The three-day stage format creates something rare in the sportive world: a journey, not just a ride. You sleep in a small Swiss village, wake up, and climb another pass. By Sunday evening, the Engadin feels like home. Yes if you want a multi-day Alpine stage experience with world-class scenery and Swiss-level organization. Yes if five passes above 2,000m through the Engadin, with cross-border adventure into Italy, is your definition of a good weekend. Skip if Swiss prices are a dealbreaker — accommodation and dining add up fast across a multi-day format. Skip if you want a competitive race environment or a field bigger than 1,500 riders. Non-negotiables: Multi-day climbing readiness — back-to-back days of 1,300-2,800m climbing with no rest day; Altitude pre-acclimatization or early arrival in the Engadin; Layering strategy tested on Alpine descents

### Eroica California
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 110 mi | 7,500 ft | Cambria, Central Coast, California, USA | September | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/eroica-california/

A challenging Central Coast gravel adventure with stunning ocean and wine country views, though event continuity and logistics require verification

### Ford RideLondon
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 100 mi | 3,900 ft | London / Essex, England | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/ford-ridelondon/

The world's greatest festival of cycling: 25,000 riders, 100 miles of closed London and Essex roads, and an Olympic legacy that raised £85 million for charity across 10 editions. A tale of two halves: iconic London landmarks on closed roads bookend a flat, fast loop through the Essex countryside. The experience is the London sections; the miles are the Essex sections. Signature challenge: The challenge is not technical or vertical — it is completing 100 miles in the company of 25,000 other riders, navigating the dense peloton safely, and managing your energy across a flat course where the temptation to go too fast too early is constant. The Essex headwinds on the return leg have ended many riders' centuries prematurely. Ford RideLondon was the cycling equivalent of the London Marathon — a mass-participation event where the experience of riding through the capital on closed roads mattered more than your finishing time. Born from the 2012 Olympics, it inspired half a million people to ride and raised £85 million for charity across 10 editions. The move from Surrey to Essex removed the dramatic hills but kept the unique thrill of London's streets without traffic. Now on indefinite pause, it leaves a gap that no other British cycling event can fill. If RideLondon returns from its indefinite pause, it is an automatic yes for anyone who wants to ride through central London on closed roads surrounded by 25,000 other cyclists. It is the perfect first century — flat, fully supported, brilliantly organized. It is not an event for riders seeking physical challenge, technical terrain, or competitive racing. It is an event for riders who want an unforgettable experience on two wheels in one of the world's great cities. Non-negotiables: 100-mile endurance base with at least two training rides of 80+ miles; Group riding confidence in a dense peloton of mixed abilities; Nutrition strategy tested over 5+ hours of sustained effort

### GFNY Salinas
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 65 mi | 2,881 ft | Salinas, Santa Elena, Ecuador | March | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gfny-salinas/

Inaugural GFNY opener on Ecuador's stunning Pacific coast with summit finish spectacle.

### GFNY San Juan
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 96 mi | 4,265 ft | San Juan, Argentina | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gfny-san-juan/

GFNY's Andean gateway: accessible distance, scenic climbs, pro experience. Two laps through Ullum Valley's plains and hills, climbing to Punta Negra dam via spectacular Interlagos route, finishing in San Juan city center. Transitions from flatlands to Andean foothills on smooth pavement. Signature challenge: Double lap structure in Ullum Valley culminating at Punta Negra dam - power endurance meets Andean backdrop.

### Gran Fondo San Diego
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 101 mi | 6,600 ft | San Diego, California, USA | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-san-diego/

A well-organized, community-focused century ride through San Diego's scenic backcountry with excellent support infrastructure and celebratory atmosphere. The Gran Fondo San Diego features the classic 'Great Western Loop' through East County's scenic backcountry, combining rolling terrain with strategic climbing challenges. The route showcases San Diego's diverse athletic landscape and natural beauty, transitioning from urban downtown San Diego through suburban areas into the rugged backcountry before returning to the waterfront finish. Signature challenge: The timed Japatul Road climb at mile 36, which determines KOM/QOM winners across eight age categories and represents the event's primary competitive climbing test.

### Lost River Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 85 mi | 8,500 ft | Mathias, West Virginia | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/lost-river-classic/

West Virginia's humid, hilly reminder that 8,500 feet of climbing doesn't need a single big mountain to break your legs. The Lost River Classic is Appalachian gravel distilled: constant elevation change through George Washington National Forest roads with no flat recovery sections. You're either grinding uphill on loose gravel or managing rocky descents that punish lapses in attention. The 85-mile course accumulates 8,500 feet via repeated moderate climbs rather than single dramatic ascents. Rocky, rutted descents with embedded rocks demand strong bike handling even when legs are cooked. Remote backcountry sections deliver genuine adventure feel with limited cell service and sparse support. This isn't a race with signature viewpoints or Instagrammable moments. It's 85 miles of honest work where heat management matters more than power output. Signature challenge: Managing 6-8 hours of constant elevation change and heat stress in August humidity with 30-mile self-supported sections. Not a single dramatic moment but sustained grinding endurance. The Lost River Classic doesn't pretend to be something it isn't. This is grassroots West Virginia gravel at its core: 85 miles of constant climbing through humid forest roads, rocky descents that punish mistakes, and August heat that compounds every effort. You won't find prize purses or Instagram influencers here. You'll find 250 regional riders who showed up to suffer together and support trail advocacy. The course delivers legitimate challenge—8,500 feet of elevation isn't a typo—but the real test is managing heat stress during 6-8 hours of constant work. Aid stations are 30 miles apart. Cell service is spotty. If something goes wrong, you problem-solve or walk out. That's the deal. If you want a well-produced national event with all the amenities, look elsewhere. If you want authentic Appalachian gravel racing where the challenge is real and the community actually cares about trail access, this is it. You need legitimate climbing fitness and wide-range gearing for the constant Appalachian rollers. August heat plus humidity makes this harder than the elevation suggests, and the rocky technical descents will punish weak bike handling. Show up undertrained and you'll be walking plenty of those punchy climbs in the remote backcountry sections.

### Munsterland Giro
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 78 mi | 2,953 ft | Munster, North Rhine-Westphalia, Germany | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/munsterland-giro/

Germany's largest amateur cycling sportive — up to 7,500 riders through the Munsterland, finishing at Munster's Schlossplatz alongside a UCI 1.Pro professional race on German Unity Day. A flat to gently rolling sportive through the Munsterland region of North Rhine-Westphalia, with three distance options designed for mass participation. The routes radiate out from a rotating start city through pastoral park landscape, Baumberge hills, and the edge of the Teutoburg Forest before converging on the iconic Schlossplatz finish in central Munster. The course changes annually as start cities rotate through Munsterland districts, but the character remains constant — wide German roads through gentle countryside with a festival finish. Signature challenge: Wind across the open Munsterland. The course may be flat, but the exposure to North German wind transforms gentle terrain into a genuine endurance test. The Munsterland offers no shelter — no hills, no forests, no buildings — just flat agricultural landscape where a 25 km/h headwind adds 30 minutes to your finishing time and strips the romance from pastoral cycling. The Sparkassen Munsterland Giro is the kind of event that makes you appreciate what German organizational efficiency can do with a flat landscape and a public holiday. Up to 7,500 riders on three distances through the Munsterland, finishing at a Baroque palace, followed by watching WorldTour sprinters contest the same finish line — all on German Unity Day. The terrain will not challenge your climbing legs. The wind might challenge your soul. But the combination of massive amateur participation, UCI professional racing, a 5,000-square-meter cycling trade fair, and Munster's deep-rooted cycling culture creates an experience that transcends the simplicity of the course. This is not a ride you do for the suffering. This is a ride you do for the spectacle, the community, and the singular pleasure of finishing at the Schlossplatz just hours before Jasper Philipsen does the same. If you want to experience Germany's cycling culture at its most concentrated — absolutely. The Munsterland Giro is the country's biggest amateur cycling event in its most cycling-obsessed city, on a national holiday, with WorldTour professionals racing the same roads. If you need mountains, vertical, or technical terrain — this is not your event. If you are visiting from outside Europe, combine it with the Velothon Berlin, a Rhine cycling trip, or Cologne cycling events to build a worthwhile German cycling tour. Non-negotiables: Group riding competence; Wind management strategy; Base endurance for chosen distance

### Pan Celtic Ultra
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 1427 mi | 84,645 ft | Wales to Ireland | July | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/pan-celtic-ultra/

An epic, culturally-immersive ultra-distance cycling odyssey through Celtic nations for experienced riders seeking adventure over competition.

### Prosecco Cycling (Gran Fondo Prosecco)
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 62 mi | 5,118 ft | Valdobbiadene, Veneto, Italy | September | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/prosecco-cycling/

100 kilometers through UNESCO vineyards, Prosecco at the aid stations, Nessun Dorma at the start line. The only gran fondo where the feed zone serves shrimp scampi and sparkling wine. A 100 km circuit through the UNESCO World Heritage Prosecco Hills, starting and finishing in Valdobbiadene. The terrain is relentlessly rolling — short, punchy climbs through terraced vineyards connected by valley-floor sections through medieval villages. The climbing is concentrated in sharp, steep pitches rather than sustained alpine passes. The Muro di Ca' del Poggio is the signature effort: 1.1 km at 12.7% average with a 19% maximum gradient, a Giro d'Italia climb compressed into a furious sprint. Between the four timed climbs, the route meanders through Combai, Follina, Tarzo, Resera, Refrontolo, San Pietro di Feletto, Conegliano, and Collalto before returning via Sernaglia della Battaglia, Col San Martino, and Guia. The rhythm is climb-descend-roll-climb, with the rolling sections offering UNESCO vineyard panoramas at every turn. The route is designed for experience first, suffering second. Signature challenge: The Muro di Ca' del Poggio — 1.1 km of 12.7% average gradient with a 19% maximum, certified by the Italian Cycling Federation and featured in the Giro d'Italia. It is the kind of climb that sorts riders into those who can hold 300+ watts for 5 minutes and those who cannot. The 19% pitches are the steepest gradient most amateur cyclists will ever ride on paved road. The reward at the top — Prosecco and Scampi at the San Pietro di Feletto ristoro — is the most Italian thing that has ever happened at a cycling aid station. Prosecco Cycling is the only gran fondo in the world where the aid stations serve sparkling wine and shrimp scampi. That sentence alone should tell you what kind of event this is. Starting with Puccini's Nessun Dorma blasting across Valdobbiadene's central piazza while waiters wave Prosecco bottles at 2,700 cyclists, the 100 km route threads through a UNESCO World Heritage vineyard landscape during the grape harvest, climbs the Giro d'Italia's Muro di Ca' del Poggio (19% maximum gradient, 1.1 km of pure punishment), and returns to a town-wide celebration of everything that makes Italian cycling culture irreplaceable. The riding is honest but not brutal. The scenery is extraordinary. The food and wine are non-negotiable. This is not an endurance test — it is Italy's end-of-season cycling party, and everyone is invited. Yes if you want the most uniquely Italian cycling experience available — wine, food, UNESCO scenery, and Puccini at the start line. Yes if EUR58 for a ride through a World Heritage landscape with Prosecco at the aid stations sounds like the best deal in European cycling. Yes if the Muro di Ca' del Poggio (19% max, Giro d'Italia climb) is on your bucket list. Skip if you need 150+ km and 3,000+ meters of climbing — this is 100 km and 1,560m, deliberately approachable. Skip if you want a pure competitive environment — all bike types are welcome and the atmosphere is celebratory. Non-negotiables: Steep-gradient climbing power — ability to sustain 300+ watts on 15-19% gradients; Repeated short-climb endurance — 8-10 punchy climbs in a single ride; Fueling discipline — practice eating and drinking on the bike at Italian aid-station scale

### Race Around Ireland
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 1336 mi | 72,000 ft | Trim, County Meath, Ireland | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/race-around-ireland/

Brutal non-stop Irish coastal ultra for true endurance masochists.

### RADL GRVL
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 81 mi | 8,202 ft | Adelaide Hills, South Australia | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/radl-grvl/

Australia's gravel monument that kicks off the international season with Adelaide Hills punishment and post-race shiraz. 130 km, 2,500 m. 85% gravel, 15% dirt. Adelaide Hills—rolling Australian bush. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: 130 km + 2,500 m cumulative elevation. Rolling Australian bush. RADL GRVL is Adelaide Hills adventure—130 km, 2,500 m elevation. Founded ~2020. 85% gravel, 15% dirt. Rolling Australian bush terrain. 400-600 riders. Adelaide city (good airport access). April autumn conditions (12-22°C). Affordable entry (A$150-A$200 / $100-$135). If you want Australian gravel adventure with good logistics, this is it. If you want local/domestic race, skip it. International destination race. If you can handle 2,300 meters of climbing on sketchy gravel roads while jetlagged, the Blue course rewards masochists with proper suffering through wine country. The Red gives you 60% of the pain for half the bragging rights. Skip it if you need perfect organization—this is only three years old and still figuring things out.

### Redlands Bicycle Classic — Legends' Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 70 mi | 5,700 ft | Redlands, San Bernardino County, California, USA | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/redlands-bicycle-classic/

40 years of where legends are born. Now ride the same roads yourself. Southern California climbing roads through citrus country and oak-studded foothills, punctuated by the iconic professional race climbs. The Gran Route combines 70 miles of Sunset Loop, Oak Glen, and Walnut climbs with a gravel sector and the downtown criterium circuit. The course rewards consistent climbing power over 5,700 ft of elevation gain in warm SoCal conditions. Signature challenge: Oak Glen — the sustained climb through apple orchards that has been the GC battleground of the Redlands Bicycle Classic for 40 years. The same road, the same gradient, the same afternoon sun. The Redlands Bicycle Classic has launched more professional cycling careers than any other American race. Since 1985, the Sunset Loop, Oak Glen, and downtown criterium have been the courses where American legends first proved themselves. The Legends' Fondo puts amateur riders on those same roads, led by Phil Gaimon, finishing at the iconic RBC finish line. This isn't about the climbing or the distance — it's about 40 years of history seeping through the asphalt. Ride Oak Glen and you're riding where the next Olympic cyclist might be racing that same weekend. Yes if you value American cycling history and want to ride iconic professional race courses. Yes if you want a well-organized SoCal weekend with professional racing to watch. Skip if you want dramatic scenery or extreme physical challenge. Non-negotiables: Climbing endurance for sustained efforts on Oak Glen and repeated short steep climbs; Heat preparation for SoCal spring conditions; Bike handling for mixed surface and criterium-style riding

### Round Denmark Bike Race
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 1221 mi | 28,409 ft | Denmark (circumnavigation) | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/round-denmark-bike-race/

Ultimate unsupported circumnavigation of Denmark testing endurance, navigation, and self-reliance.

### Tour de Okinawa
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 124 mi | 6,560 ft | Nago City, Okinawa, Japan | November | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-de-okinawa/

Japan's biggest citizen road race — subtropical island, shut-down roads, Asian amateur racing at its finest. Deceptively demanding. The two cross-island climbs through Yanbaru forest are the race's signature — honest 5-8% gradients through subtropical canopy that shatter groups and determine results. Between those climbs, the constant coastal undulations from sea level create cumulative fatigue that the raw elevation numbers disguise. The course follows the coastline of northern Okinawa, cutting twice across the island's spine where the mountains live. It is not a mountain course, but it is not flat either — 2,000 meters of climbing from sea level is 2,000 meters of absolute gain. Signature challenge: The accumulation. Neither climb alone is extraordinary, but the combination of subtropical humidity, constant undulation, and two honest forest climbs creates progressive fatigue that catches riders who went out too hard on the flat coastal opening. The race rewards patience and punishes aggression. Tour de Okinawa is the race that Japanese amateur cyclists build their entire season around. It is not the hardest course, not the deepest field by global standards, and not the most internationally recognized. What it is: the most professionally organized citizen road race in Asia, held on a subtropical island with fully closed roads, wrapped in Okinawan hospitality, and steeped in 35 years of racing history. The two Yanbaru climbs separate the strong from the ambitious, and the November timing makes it the perfect season capstone. For Japanese cyclists, this needs no justification — it is the season finale that defines your year. For international riders, Tour de Okinawa rewards those willing to navigate the logistics: you get a UCI-grade racing experience on a subtropical island with some of the best on-course support in amateur cycling. The two Yanbaru climbs are honest and the coastal roads are stunning. Come for the race, stay for the sashimi. Non-negotiables: Humidity acclimation sessions in weeks 6-10; Back-to-back climbing sessions with cumulative volume; 4-5 hour dress rehearsal with sustained pace and fueling protocol

### UCI Gran Fondo Hangzhou
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 87 mi | 5,200 ft | Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/uci-gran-fondo-hangzhou/

Scenic Qiandao Lake UCI qualifier blending Chinese beauty with competitive road punch.

### Adelaide Epic Ride (Santos Tour Down Under)
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 85 mi | 5,906 ft | Adelaide, South Australia, Australia | January | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/adelaide-epic-ride/

136 km on a UCI WorldTour stage route through the Adelaide Hills with 1,800 m of climbing, ridden on the same day as the pros. The community ride of Australia's biggest professional cycling race. A sealed-road journey from the Adelaide coast through the southern suburbs and into the Adelaide Hills, following the exact route of the UCI WorldTour Men's Stage 3. The 136 km route begins at Henley Beach at sea level and heads south and east through Adelaide's suburban fringe before the terrain tilts decisively upward into the Adelaide Hills. The character shifts dramatically across the ride: flat coastal kilometres give way to rolling suburban hills, which in turn yield to the punchy categorised climbs that define the stage. Chandlers Hill arrives first — a short, sharp shock to legs that have been spinning on flat road. Wickham Hill follows, a more sustained effort through Adelaide Hills bushland. The final act is Mount Barker Summit, arriving with less than 5 km to the finish in Nairne, when accumulated fatigue and January heat conspire to make every gradient feel twice as steep. The descents between climbs are fast and rewarding — wide, well-surfaced roads swept to professional racing standards. Signature challenge: Mount Barker Summit with less than 5 km to the finish — the final categorised climb after 130+ km of riding and 1,500+ metres of accumulated climbing in January heat. Every pedal stroke on this climb is earned. The gradient is honest, the heat is real, and the finish at Nairne is close enough to be cruel. The Adelaide Epic Ride is the rare community ride that lets you put your wheels on a genuine UCI WorldTour stage route and ride it on the same day as the professionals. The 136 km route from Henley Beach to Nairne through the Adelaide Hills delivers 1,800 metres of honest climbing — Chandlers Hill, Wickham Hill, and Mount Barker Summit are real climbs, not token hills — under the full force of South Australian summer heat. This is not the hardest sportive in Australia. But it is the one most directly connected to the highest level of professional cycling, and that connection is what makes it special. Yes if you want the unique experience of riding a genuine WorldTour stage route on race day, embedded in the Santos Tour Down Under festival atmosphere. Yes if you can handle South Australian summer heat and want a well-supported 136 km ride with real climbing. Skip if you need competitive racing, if you cannot tolerate extreme heat, or if you find $225 AUD steep for a community ride. Non-negotiables: Heat adaptation — minimum two weeks of training in hot conditions before the event; One training ride of 100+ km with 1,200+ metres of climbing in warm conditions; Hydration strategy tested and proven — 500-750 ml per hour plus electrolytes

### Crystal Bear
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 192 mi | 8,000 ft | Laona, WI to Crystal Falls, MI | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/crystal-bear/

Crystal Bear is what happens when bikepacking meets gravel racing in the Northwoods—darkness, sandy sections, and 192 miles of self-reliant suffering. The Crystal Bear is an overnight point-to-point bikepacking odyssey through the Northwoods of Wisconsin and Michigan's Upper Peninsula. Unlike mass-start gravel races, this is self-supported adventure racing: you carry overnight gear, navigate independently, and resupply at small-town gas stations 40-60 miles apart. The terrain itself is moderate—rolling Northwoods gravel with 8,000 feet spread across 192 miles—but the overnight format is the real challenge. You'll spend 8-10 hours riding through pitch-black forest with only your headlight for company. Sandy sections sap momentum. Stream crossings require focus. Cold overnight temperatures (30-45°F) demand warmth management. Sleep deprivation compounds navigation challenges. This isn't about crushing segments or chasing KOMs. It's about holding it together mentally and physically for 15-30 hours straight while entirely self-reliant. Signature challenge: Managing 8-10 hours of solo darkness riding through remote Northwoods forest while fighting sleep deprivation, cold, and psychological isolation with zero external support. The Crystal Bear isn't about who's fastest. It's about who can hold it together when every external factor—darkness, cold, loneliness, sleep deprivation—is screaming to quit. This is overnight bikepacking racing at its purest: 192 miles of self-supported Northwoods gravel where you'll spend 8-10 hours riding solo through pitch-black forest with nothing but your headlight and your own thoughts. The terrain itself isn't brutal—8,000 feet over 192 miles is moderate climbing—but the format is the challenge. You'll fight sandy sections that kill momentum. You'll navigate stream crossings at 2 AM when your brain is mush. You'll stop to eat and start shivering within 5 minutes from overnight cold. And you'll do it all alone, with resupply points 40-60 miles apart and spotty cell service. This isn't a race for your first ultra-endurance event. It's for riders who've done the long stuff and are ready to test themselves against isolation and darkness. If you finish, you'll have earned something real. If you DNF, you'll learn exactly where your mental limits are. Either way, you won't forget it. You need overnight riding experience and the gear to match—this isn't your first gravel race with a bivy thrown in last minute. The sandy Wisconsin forest roads will humble roadie legs, and navigation errors in darkness can add hours. Come if you've done multi-day rides solo and understand that 'self-supported' means exactly that when you're flat-tired at 2 AM in the middle of nowhere.

### Dirty French
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 100 mi | 9,000 ft | Chillicothe, Ohio | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/dirty-french/

Ohio delivers 9,000 feet of legitimate gravel suffering without the pretension of mountain racing. Dirty French borrows everything cruel from Ohio's rolling farm country and amplifies it through 9,000 feet of grinding climbs. This isn't manicured gravel—it's crushed limestone that turns to peanut butter when wet, farm roads that pitch up without warning, and long stretches where your GPS becomes your only friend. The Chillicothe region doesn't negotiate; it just keeps rolling upward until your legs forget what flat feels like. Signature challenge: Nine thousand feet of elevation over Ohio farm roads means you're climbing constantly, but never dramatically enough to justify walking. The gradient hovers in that cruel 6-8% range where you can't rest but can't power through either. By mile 70, every slight rise feels personal. You need solid fitness for 100 miles and 9,000 feet—this isn't a beginner's ride. The rolling Midwest terrain means constant power output with no real recovery sections. Come prepared for variable weather and mechanical self-sufficiency. If you can handle a 6-8 hour suffer-fest without mountain views as motivation, this will test you properly.
