### Le Race
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 62.1 mi | 6,070 ft | Christchurch to Akaroa, Canterbury, New Zealand | March | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/le-race/

New Zealand's one-day road classic. 100 km across an extinct volcano. Olympic medalists in the Hall of Fame. Six hundred riders. One French harbour town. A volcanic crossing, not a circuit. Le Race is a 100 km point-to-point race that traverses Banks Peninsula — the eroded remnant of two extinct shield volcanoes — from Christchurch to Akaroa. The course climbs from sea level to the Port Hills ridgeline, traverses exposed summit roads with 360-degree panoramas, descends to the Little River valley, climbs back up to the Hilltop crater rim, and plunges down to the French harbour village of Akaroa at sea level. The terrain is relentless: you are climbing, descending, or fighting wind on an exposed ridge for virtually the entire 100 km. There is almost no flat road. The course rewards climbers, punishes poor descenders, and exposes anyone who has not prepared for crosswinds on volcanic ridgelines. Signature challenge: The Hilltop climb is the centrepiece, but Le Race's true challenge is the accumulation: Dyers Pass, Summit Road crosswinds, Gebbies Pass descent, the Hilltop grind, more Summit Road, and the steep Long Bay descent — all compressed into 100 km with nearly 1,900 metres of climbing. There is no recovery. There is no flat road. The course is designed by volcanic geology, not by committee, and it does not care about your legs. Michael Vink's course record of 2:37 means averaging nearly 40 km/h with 1,900m of climbing — a mark that shows how brutally honest this course is. Le Race is New Zealand's one-day road cycling classic — 100 kilometres from Cathedral Square in earthquake-rebuilt Christchurch to the French harbour village of Akaroa, across the crater rim of an extinct volcanic complex that juts into the Pacific Ocean. The route climbs Dyers Pass out of the city, traverses the exposed Summit Road with panoramic views of Lyttelton Harbour and the Southern Alps, descends to Little River, then grinds back up to the Hilltop — 30-50 minutes of sustained climbing to the Banks Peninsula crater rim — before plunging down to the French-flavoured finish. The Hall of Fame includes Olympic medalists, world champions, and Commonwealth Games winners. Nate Pringle's 2024 victory — cramping on the final climb, squeezing out one last attack to hold off the chasing bunch — is the kind of desperate, decisive racing this course produces. Six hundred riders line up each March for New Zealand's most prestigious day on a road bike. Yes if you want New Zealand's most prestigious one-day road cycling experience with genuine competitive racing on a world-class course. Yes if you are drawn to volcanic landscapes, dramatic coastal ridgelines, and a finish in the most charming village in New Zealand. Skip if you need a large-field mass-participation atmosphere — this is 600 riders, not 6,000. Skip if the 24+ hour journey to Christchurch from the Northern Hemisphere is a dealbreaker. Non-negotiables: Climbing endurance — comfortable with 1,900m of climbing over 100 km with no meaningful flat sections; Confident descending on steep, technical roads with tight switchbacks; Crosswind riding ability on exposed ridgelines

### Levi's GranFondo
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 103 mi | 8,500 ft | Santa Rosa, Sonoma County, California, USA | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/levis-granfondo/

Levi Leipheimer's backyard roads. King Ridge through the redwoods. 103 miles of Sonoma County's finest. A greatest-hits reel of Sonoma County cycling compressed into 103 miles. The route oscillates between three distinct landscapes — sun-drenched wine country, dark redwood forest canyons, and exposed Pacific Coast ridgeline — with enough climbing to keep the legs honest throughout. The climbing is punchy and repeated rather than sustained Alpine grinding. King Ridge is the centerpiece: a 4-mile effort through cathedral redwoods that defines the entire ride. Signature challenge: King Ridge through the coastal redwoods. The climb itself is honest — 4+ miles at 5-9% — but the setting elevates it beyond mere physical effort. The transition from dark forest to sunlit Pacific ridgeline is one of American cycling's great moments. Levi's GranFondo is the best gran fondo in the western US, and it's not particularly close. The King Ridge climb through coastal redwoods is the reason — a 4-mile sustained effort through cathedral groves that opens onto Pacific Coast views, giving you the kind of ride-defining moment that most US sportives simply cannot manufacture. The rest of the route is a greatest-hits tour of Sonoma County: vineyard-lined roads, rolling wine country, and enough climbing to make 103 miles feel significantly harder than the distance suggests. It lacks the Alpine gravitas of European events, but what it delivers — variety, scenery, and a well-run experience — is genuinely excellent. Yes if you want Northern California's premier cycling experience with world-class scenery and variety. Yes if King Ridge through the redwoods and Pacific Coast views sound like your kind of riding. Skip if you need Alpine-scale climbing or international competitive prestige. Skip if Sonoma County wine country pricing doesn't fit your budget. Non-negotiables: Back-to-back long rides of 80-100 miles with 5,000+ feet of climbing; Repeated short climb intervals (5-15 minutes at threshold); Full dress rehearsal — 80+ miles with nutrition strategy on hilly terrain

### Race Across Portugal
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 621 mi | 36,417 ft | Porto region, Portugal | March | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/race-across-portugal/

Challenging self-supported ultra-road race kicking off the 2026 season in Portugal's diverse landscapes.

### Race Around Netherlands
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 1211 mi | — | Netherlands (loop) | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/race-around-netherlands/

Epic self-supported flat loop testing endurance against Dutch winds and distance.

### Sea Otter Classic Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 90 mi | 5,500 ft | Monterey, California, USA | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/sea-otter-classic-gran-fondo/

90 miles of wine country roads wrapped inside the world's biggest cycling festival. A tale of two halves. The first 40 miles lull you into comfort through flat agricultural valley roads and vineyard aid stations. Then the Cahoon Summit climb punishes complacency with 10+ miles of sustained effort before the Laureles Grade kicker reminds you that the finish is earned, not given. Signature challenge: The Cahoon Summit climb: 10+ miles of sustained 4-6% gradient through East Carmel Valley Road, arriving after 50 miles of riding when glycogen stores are depleting. The Sea Otter Classic Gran Fondo is not the hardest ride you will do this year, and it is not trying to be. It is 90 miles of gorgeous Salinas Valley wine country wrapped inside the world's largest cycling festival, and that combination is more powerful than either element alone.

The route is honest: flat valley roads for 40 miles, then the real work begins with the Cahoon Summit grind and the steep Laureles Grade kicker to finish. 5,500 feet of climbing will remind your legs that this is a proper fondo, not a parade. But the four vineyard-set aid stations, the April coastal weather, and the knowledge that Laguna Seca Raceway awaits at the finish soften the suffering.

What makes this ride special is everything surrounding it. A 4-day festival pass comes with registration. 1,000+ bike brands at the expo. 14 race disciplines across the weekend. Cycling legends signing autographs. It is the season opener for American cycling, and riding the gran fondo puts you at the center of it. Yes if you want the ultimate cycling culture weekend with a beautiful ride attached. Skip if you need competitive racing, chip times, or want to avoid $300/night hotels. Non-negotiables: Century-distance endurance base; Sustained climbing preparation; Fueling strategy for back-loaded course

### Tour de Brisbane
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 68.3 mi | 4,068 ft | Brisbane, Queensland, Australia | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-de-brisbane/

Innovative urban gran fondo with excellent logistics but modest climbing and prestige. Tour de Brisbane transforms urban infrastructure into a cycling playground, starting at City Botanic Gardens and weaving through Brisbane's heart on fully closed roads. The course loops through the city center, crosses the Brisbane River via major bridges, navigates through tunnels and expressways, and features five technical turns requiring constant attention. Mount Coot-tha provides the only significant climbing challenge, while the remainder emphasizes urban navigation, tactical positioning, and the unique experience of riding through normally car-filled streets. The finish returns to South Bank Cultural Precinct, completing a comprehensive tour of Brisbane's cycling-friendly infrastructure. Signature challenge: Mount Coot-tha's 2km climb at 9% gradient serves as the race's defining moment, arriving at 67km to determine final positioning. While modest by gran fondo standards, the climb's placement late in the event and its role as the course's only significant elevation gain make it tactically crucial. The summit's panoramic Brisbane views reward the effort before the final descent to South Bank finish. Tour de Brisbane represents a genuinely innovative approach to gran fondo racing—taking the closed-road concept into a vibrant city rather than rural mountains. The event delivers on its promise of fully closed urban infrastructure, excellent logistics, and strong community engagement. However, it's fundamentally a different animal than traditional gran fondos: flatter, shorter, and more about urban cycling experience than climbing or distance. For Australian cyclists or those seeking a unique international cycling destination, it's compelling. For riders chasing elevation, prestige, or classic gran fondo suffering, look elsewhere. Yes if you value unique urban cycling experiences, seek UCI Worlds qualification, or want to explore Brisbane's cycling culture. Skip if you're chasing serious elevation, classic gran fondo suffering, or established prestige—this isn't that event. Non-negotiables: Build aerobic base with 8-10 hours weekly cycling volume; Practice sustained 9% climbing at race pace; Complete at least one 100km+ training ride at race intensity

### Tour de Yabakei
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 62 mi | 3,500 ft | Nakatsu, Oita, Japan | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-de-yabakei/

Scenic Yabakei gorge ramble blending climbs and culture.

### Turkcell Granfondo Izmir
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 65 mi | — | Izmir, Turkey | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/turkcell-granfondo-izmir/

Scenic Aegean Coast gran fondo in Turkey's cycling hub with growing popularity and solid organization.

### Vätternrundan
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 196 mi | 4,593 ft | Motala, Östergötland, Sweden | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/vatternrundan/

315 km around Lake Vättern through the Swedish midsummer night. 22,000 riders. No results. No rankings. Pure cycling. Vätternrundan is not a race. It has never been a race. Since 1966, when a sports medicine physician and a bike shop owner sent 334 riders around Lake Vättern to study what happens to the human body over 315 km, it has been a personal challenge wrapped in a Nordic ritual. There are no results. No rankings. No prizes. There is only the lake, the midsummer night, and the 22,000 other riders sharing the road.

The sun barely sets — the sky glows amber and indigo through the night hours, never reaching true darkness this close to the solstice. You ride through sleeping villages, past midsummer bonfires, past fields of barley lit by twilight. At midnight, you eat hot soup in a Swedish countryside service station with strangers who will become friends by dawn. At 3:30 AM, the sun rises over the eastern shore and you ride the final 80 km home to Motala with the lake on your left and 230 km in your legs.

It is 315 km of gently rolling road around a lake. And it is one of the most extraordinary experiences in cycling. Yes if you want an ultra-distance experience unlike anything else in cycling — the midsummer night is genuinely magical. Yes if you value community and shared adventure over competition. Yes if you are pursuing the Swedish Classic Circuit. Skip if you want to race — there are no results. Skip if you need dramatic terrain — this is flat Swedish farmland around a lake. Skip if remote Scandinavian logistics are a dealbreaker. Non-negotiables: Progressive long rides reaching 200+ km in training; Night riding experience — minimum 3 rides of 2+ hours in darkness or twilight; Tested nutrition plan for 12+ hours — including hot food and caffeine strategy

### Vietnam Cycling Challenge
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 62 mi | 4,921 ft | Da Lat, Lam Dong, Vietnam | November | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/vietnam-cycling-challenge/

Scenic highland road challenge in Vietnam's cool Central Highlands.

### United Energy Around the Bay
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 137 mi | 2,812 ft | Melbourne, Victoria, Australia | October | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/around-the-bay-in-a-day/

210 km around Port Phillip Bay with 10,000 riders and a ferry. Australia's biggest one-day cycling event, and a Melbourne institution since 1993. A flat, coastal circumnavigation of Port Phillip Bay on sealed roads and paths, punctuated by a 40-minute ferry crossing between Queenscliff and Sorrento. The Classic route runs anti-clockwise: west from Albert Park over the West Gate Bridge, through Werribee and Little River to Geelong, along the Bellarine Peninsula to Queenscliff, across the water by ferry to Sorrento, then back along the Mornington Peninsula and Beach Road to the finish at Albert Park. The terrain is relentlessly flat — the challenge is distance, wind, and endurance rather than gradient. The scenery shifts from urban Melbourne to industrial western suburbs, open farmland, Geelong's waterfront, the coastal Bellarine Peninsula, and finally the iconic Beach Road run home. Signature challenge: The sheer distance — 210 km of flat coastal riding with no hills to break the monotony and no shelter from the wind. The western shore between Werribee and Geelong is where Around the Bay reveals itself: in a headwind, this section is a war of attrition. The ferry provides a 40-minute reprieve, but you still have 80 km to ride on the other side. Around the Bay is not the hardest ride in Australia. It is not the hilliest, the most technical, or the most remote. It is the biggest, the most inclusive, and the most culturally embedded cycling event on the continent. Ten thousand riders circumnavigating Port Phillip Bay on a single Sunday in October, crossing the water by ferry, finishing on Beach Road as the afternoon light hits the bay — this is Australian cycling at its most democratic. The flat terrain means the challenge is pure endurance. The 210 km distance means it is not trivial. The community means you never ride alone. Yes if you want to experience Australia's largest cycling event in one of the world's great cities. Yes if you value community over competition and want a well-supported 210 km endurance challenge on flat terrain. Skip if you need climbing, technical terrain, or a competitive racing environment. Non-negotiables: Minimum one 160+ km training ride at target pace; Wind training — sustained efforts in crosswind and headwind conditions; Nutrition strategy tested on a long ride — 200+ calories per hour for 7+ hours

### Atlas Mountain Race
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 750 mi | 80,000 ft | Morocco | February | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/atlas-mountain-race/

Moroccan Atlas Mountains ultra-endurance gravel race. Atlas Mountains ultra-endurance self-supported gravel. Signature challenge: 750 miles and 80,000 feet through Atlas Mountains. Atlas Mountain Race is an ultra-endurance self-supported race through Morocco—750 miles with 80,000 feet of climbing. The Atlas Mountains are stunning and the adventure is extreme, but the very difficult access, extreme conditions, high costs, and self-supported format keep it in Tier 3. If you're an elite ultra-endurance rider with a big budget—maybe. For most riders—there are better options. Non-negotiables: Ultra-endurance training; Self-sufficiency skills; Budget planning

### Best Buddies Challenge: Miami
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 100 mi | 500 ft | Miami, Florida, USA | November | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/best-buddies-challenge-miami/

Luxury charity peloton ride with pros through Miami's icons.

### Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Ride
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 77 mi | 3,796 ft | Geelong, Victoria, Australia | January | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cadel-evans-great-ocean-road-ride/

Cadel Evans' legacy. The Great Ocean Road. Challambra Crescent at 10%. Ride the pro course the same weekend. A coastal sportive with a sharp sting. The Great Ocean Road sections provide the visual spectacle — crashing surf, dramatic coastal cliffs, wide Pacific views — while the Surf Coast hinterland delivers the physical challenge through relentless short rollers. The route mimics the professional race course, with Challambra Crescent as the decisive climb. It's not a mountain sportive, but the accumulated climbing, the heat, and the coastal wind make 153 km harder than the distance suggests. Signature challenge: Challambra Crescent. At 1.5 km, it's over before it starts — but 10%+ gradients on tired legs, in the heat, on the same tarmac that WorldTour professionals contest, make it the most meaningful 1.5 km of the day. The Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Ride trades on two things: a legendary name and a legendary road. It delivers on both. Riding the same course that WorldTour professionals race the same weekend — attacking Challambra Crescent where races are decided — gives this sportive a connection to elite cycling that most events can only dream of. The Great Ocean Road provides the visual payoff, and the Geelong-Surf Coast region's cycling culture creates a genuine festival atmosphere. The ride itself is moderate by gran fondo standards — 153 km with 1,600m of climbing won't break experienced riders — but the experience transcends the physical challenge. Yes if riding the same roads as WorldTour professionals appeals to you. Yes if the Great Ocean Road and the Cadel Evans connection are meaningful to your cycling fandom. Skip if you need a mountain challenge — this is a coastal sportive with moderate climbing. Skip if Australian summer heat is a dealbreaker. Non-negotiables: Heat adaptation training — riding in warm conditions regularly; Short steep climb repetitions — 1-3 minute efforts at threshold and above; Full dress rehearsal — 130+ km in warm conditions with nutrition plan

### Dirty 30
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 100 mi | 3,500 ft | Saranac, Michigan | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/dirty-30/

The Dirty 30 gets by on Michigan charm and distance options, but without signature terrain or serious prestige, it's more local weekend fun than destination race. Rolling Michigan gravel. Forest roads, rural gravel. 3,500 ft over 100 miles = constant rollers. No big climbs, just relentless ups and downs. Signature challenge: Constant rolling terrain—cumulative elevation without big climbs. April cold and mud risk. Dirty 30 is a Michigan Gravel Race Series event in April—meaning cold starts, mud potential, and early season fitness reality checks. 100 miles, 3,500 ft of constant rollers (no big climbs, just relentless ups and downs). Field is ~500, vibe is regional community. This isn't a destination race; it's a solid early season test for Midwest riders. If you're from Michigan or nearby, it's a good value ($45-$75). If you're flying in, there are better options. If you're in the Midwest looking for solid training miles with low pressure, this works. The 100-mile option gives you century bragging rights without the Unbound brutality, and starting from downtown Saranac beats another cornfield parking lot. Skip it if you need technical challenges or want to test yourself against serious competition—3500 feet over 100 miles won't break anyone who's been riding longer than six months.

### Etape Loch Ness
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 66 mi | 2,953 ft | Inverness, Scottish Highlands | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/etape-loch-ness/

Scotland's largest closed-road sportive — 66 miles around one of the world's most iconic lochs with 900m of climbing and a King of the Mountain at 12%. A 360-degree loop around one of the world's most famous bodies of water on fully closed roads. The route departs Inverness heading west along the north shore of Loch Ness on the A82, following the loch through the Great Glen — a geological fault line that splits Scotland diagonally from Inverness to Fort William. At Fort Augustus, the route reaches the southern tip of the loch where the Caledonian Canal connects Loch Ness to Loch Oich. Here, riders face the King of the Mountain: a 4.8-mile timed climb above the village gaining 380 metres with gradients reaching 12%. The summit delivers Highland panoramas before the descent to the south shore. The return leg follows narrower, quieter B-roads along the south side of Loch Ness — more atmospheric, more exposed to wind, and with rolling terrain that keeps the legs honest. The final miles approach Inverness from the south, with the last section through the city operating on left-lane closure rather than full road closure. The finish returns riders to the event area near Eden Court, completing the circumnavigation. Signature challenge: The King of the Mountain at Glen Doe above Fort Augustus. 4.8 miles gaining 380 metres with gradients reaching 12%. The only timed competitive element in a non-competitive sportive, it is where the Etape Loch Ness transforms from a scenic ride into a proper cycling challenge. Etape Loch Ness is what happens when you take one of the world's most famous natural landmarks, close the roads around it, and let 6,000 cyclists loose at dawn. The result is a sportive that trades on scenery and atmosphere rather than raw difficulty, and it delivers on that promise completely. The 66-mile loop around Loch Ness on fully closed roads is a cycling experience that few UK events can match — the loch emerging from morning mist, the Highland mountains rising on either side, Fort Augustus at the halfway point, and the King of the Mountain climb to test your legs and your resolve. This is not a race that will break elite cyclists, but it is an experience that will stay with every rider who does it. The £2 million raised for Macmillan Cancer Support gives it a soul that pure sporting events often lack. Yes, if riding closed roads around Loch Ness at dawn with 6,000 cyclists appeals to you as an experience. Yes, if you want a well-organized sportive that welcomes all abilities while still offering a legitimate climbing challenge. Skip if you need serious competitive racing depth — this is participatory. Skip if sub-100km on paved roads doesn't challenge you. And check the weather forecast before packing — Scottish April weather demands respect and proper kit. Non-negotiables: KOM climbing fitness — sustained 20-40 minute efforts at threshold on 6-12% gradients; 70+ mile endurance base with 800m+ climbing in a single ride; Cold and wet weather riding experience — at least 3 long rides in sub-10°C and rain

### Fuji Panorama Gran Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 75 mi | 5,906 ft | Lake Saiko, Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan | — | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/fuji-panorama-gran-fondo/

Stunning Fuji-framed road loops for scenery chasers seeking accessible gran fondo suffering.

### Gran Fondo Firenze
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 83 mi | 6,562 ft | Florence, Tuscany, Italy | April | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-firenze/

Over the Ponte Vecchio and into the Tuscan hills — Florence turns its Renaissance streets into a starting grid and the Mugello into your proving ground. There are gran fondos with bigger climbs. There are gran fondos with bigger fields. There are gran fondos with more prestige and deeper history. But there is no gran fondo that starts where this one starts. You line up at Piazzale Michelangelo with the entire Florence skyline below you — Brunelleschi's Dome, the Palazzo Vecchio, the Arno catching the morning light — and then you ride down into it. You cross the Ponte Vecchio on a bicycle. Let that sink in. The most photographed bridge in Italy, a medieval jewelers' row that has spanned the Arno since 1345, and you are riding across it in lycra with a race number pinned to your jersey. After the city releases you, the Tuscan hills take over: the climb to Fiesole through cypress groves, the Mugello's rolling countryside dotted with olive groves and stone farmhouses, the feed stations where the volunteers pour you wine alongside the energy drink.

The De Rosa Granfondo Firenze is not Italy's hardest ride or its most famous. It does not have the Muro di Sormano's 27% gradient or the Maratona's 4,000m of Dolomite climbing. What it has is Florence — a city that has defined Western civilization's relationship with beauty for six centuries — as both its starting line and its backdrop. The Via Salviati finish, where Rui Costa won the 2013 World Championship road race on a 20% gradient wall, provides the competitive punch. The Mugello countryside provides the Tuscan pastoral. And the city itself provides something no other gran fondo can offer: the feeling that you are not just racing through scenery, but through history. Pack your climbing legs and your appetite. You will need both. Yes if the idea of racing across the Ponte Vecchio and starting from Piazzale Michelangelo makes your heart beat faster. Yes if you want a challenging but accessible Italian gran fondo wrapped in world-class cultural scenery. Yes if you value the total experience — city, countryside, food, history — over pure climbing brutality or maximum competitive depth. Skip if you want an extreme mountain challenge — look to the Maratona dles Dolomites or La Marmotte instead. Skip if you need a massive, festival-atmosphere field — the Nove Colli or Granfondo Campagnolo deliver that. Skip if you prefer gravel or off-road — the GF Strade Bianche is the Tuscan alternative for dirt road lovers. Non-negotiables: Steep gradient climbing — repeated efforts at 12-20% to prepare for the Via Salviati finish; Sustained rolling endurance — 4-5 hour rides with 1,500m+ of cumulative climbing on varied terrain; Fueling practice — eating and drinking on the bike during sustained moderate efforts

### Gran Fondo Jasper
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 102 mi | 5,548 ft | Jasper, Alberta, Canada | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gran-fondo-jasper/

Canada's only national park fondo. 165 km through the Canadian Rockies. Marmot Basin — Alberta's #1 climb. The Icefields Parkway. Jasper. A cathedral on two wheels. Every route option starts and finishes in Jasper townsite, radiating outward through the Canadian Rockies like spokes from a hub. The signature Gran Fondo (165 km) heads west on Connaught Drive, picks up the Icefields Parkway, then turns onto Highway 93A before attacking Marmot Basin — a 13-14 km climb averaging 5.4-5.8% to the ski resort base lodge, ranked Alberta's #1 categorized climb. After descending Marmot, the route continues south on the Icefields Parkway toward Poboktan Creek, where glaciers and 3,000 m peaks frame the turnaround before riders retrace north to Jasper. The Forte Fondo (190 km) adds Edith Cavell Road — 19 km of narrow, winding switchbacks climbing to the Mount Edith Cavell Wilderness Hostel with views of Angel Glacier — between Marmot and the Icefields Parkway push. The Leg Burner (126 km) reaches Athabasca Falls before routing south to Goatlick on the Parkway. The Medio (100 km) includes Marmot Basin and rolling 93A terrain. The Piccolo (65 km) loops through 93A without major climbs. Throughout every distance, you are inside a UNESCO World Heritage national park surrounded by the Canadian Rockies — elk on the roadside, glaciers on the horizon, and mountain air that tastes different from anywhere else. Signature challenge: Marmot Basin Climb. Alberta's #1 categorized road climb per Canadian Cycling Magazine. 13-14 km at 5.4-5.8% average gradient with 770-828 m of elevation gain. Maximum gradient 9.3%. Featured as Stage 4 of the 2015 Tour of Alberta, won by WorldTour rider Tom-Jelte Slagter. The climb winds through subalpine forest to the Marmot Basin ski resort base lodge, with panoramic views of the Athabasca Valley from the upper reaches. Steady, scenic, and relentless — the climb that defines the event. Gran Fondo Jasper exists because no other organized cycling event in North America can put you inside a national park for 165 km of mountain riding. That is the entire value proposition, and it is enormous. The Icefields Parkway is not merely a beautiful road — it is consistently ranked among the most spectacular drives on Earth, and you get to ride it on a bicycle surrounded by glaciers, turquoise rivers, and peaks that pierce 3,000 meters. Marmot Basin — Alberta's top categorized climb, tested by WorldTour riders during the 2015 Tour of Alberta — delivers legitimate suffering on the way to the treeline. The Forte Fondo adds Edith Cavell Road for 190 km and 2,339 m of climbing through one of the Rockies' most iconic mountain approaches. This is not a race. It is 300 people riding through a cathedral of mountains in a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The 2024 wildfire that destroyed a third of Jasper adds a raw dimension — you ride through a landscape that is simultaneously spectacular and scarred, through a community that is rebuilding. The logistics are the price of admission: remote, expensive to reach, weather-dependent. But if you ride a road bike and want a single day of riding that you will remember for the rest of your life, this is it. Yes if riding through a UNESCO World Heritage national park in the Canadian Rockies is your idea of a perfect day on a bike. Yes if the Icefields Parkway, glaciers, and mountain wildlife matter more to you than competitive depth or field size. Skip if the 4-hour drive from Edmonton and limited Jasper lodging is a dealbreaker. Skip if you need a large, competitive field — this is 300 riders enjoying mountains, not 3,000 racing each other. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing fitness — 30-60 minute threshold efforts on 5-7% gradients; 100+ km endurance base with 1,200+ m climbing in a single ride; Cold-weather riding gear and rain preparation tested in training

### Granfondo Pinarello
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 98 mi | 8,200 ft | Treviso, Veneto, Italy | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-pinarello/

Pinarello's own gran fondo through the Prosecco Hills and over the Muro di Ca' del Poggio. The brand ride done right. Rolling Veneto pre-Alpine terrain punctuated by short, savage wall climbs. The route leaves Treviso and winds through the Prosecco Hills — UNESCO-protected vineyard country — before hitting the Muro di Ca' del Poggio, a Giro d'Italia wall that ramps to 18% and leaves you wondering why you didn't fit a 34-tooth cassette. The Montello climb adds a longer, forested ascent. Between the signature climbs, the terrain is rolling and fast on good Italian tarmac. Signature challenge: The Muro di Ca' del Poggio — a 1.2 km Giro d'Italia wall with ramps to 18%. Short, savage, and impossible to pace if you don't know it's coming. Granfondo Pinarello is a brand ride, and it doesn't pretend otherwise. You're riding through the Veneto countryside that shaped one of cycling's most storied manufacturers — the same roads where Pinarello has tested frames for five decades. The Muro di Ca' del Poggio is a genuine Giro landmark: a short, vicious wall that separates riders who brought the right gearing from those who didn't. The Prosecco Hills are UNESCO-lovely. The organization reflects Pinarello's brand standards. What it isn't: a monument, a classic, or an event that transcends its corporate origins. It's a very good Italian gran fondo hosted by a very famous bike company. Yes if you appreciate cycling heritage and want to ride roads shaped by one of the sport's great manufacturers. Yes if you want a well-organized Italian gran fondo with a Giro landmark climb. Skip if you want alpine epicness or an event with its own competitive identity beyond the brand. Non-negotiables: Gearing for 18% walls — 34x32 minimum, compact with 34t cassette recommended; Heat acclimatization and hydration plan for July Veneto; Punchy climb repeatability — train for short, steep efforts

### GranFondo San Sebastián
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 106 mi | 9,843 ft | San Sebastián (Donostia), Basque Country, Spain | July | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/granfondo-san-sebastian/

The amateur edition of a WorldTour classic. Same roads, same Jaizkibel, same Bay of Biscay. One day earlier. Relentless Basque terrain — constant climbing through green coastal headlands and inland valleys. The route leaves San Sebastián and winds through the Basque interior before hitting the coast and the signature ascent of Jaizkibel, an exposed headland climb overlooking the Bay of Biscay. The roads are narrow and winding. The terrain never flattens for long — classic Basque geography of short, sharp climbs connected by brief valleys. 3,000m of climbing across 170 km means you're always going up or down. Signature challenge: Jaizkibel — the exposed Atlantic headland climb that defines the Clásica San Sebastián. Wind, views, and history on a 456m coastal ascent. GranFondo San Sebastián is the rare amateur event that inherits genuine pro racing DNA. You ride the same Basque roads — including Jaizkibel's exposed Atlantic headland — that the WorldTour peloton will race the next day. That's not marketing; that's the actual route. San Sebastián as a host city is extraordinary: pintxos bars, La Concha beach, old-town atmosphere. The 170 km route with 3,000m of climbing is a proper day's work — relentless Basque rollers that never let you rest. What keeps this from the top tier is independence: strip away the pro race connection, and you have a good Basque gran fondo. But you don't need to strip it away — the connection is the point. Yes if you want to ride a WorldTour route and experience Basque cycling culture in one of Europe's best cities. Yes if you want a genuinely demanding gran fondo (170 km / 3,000m). Skip if you need sunshine guarantees or want an event with its own independent identity. Non-negotiables: Climbing endurance for 3,000m over 170 km — train relentless vertical; Rain gear and wet-weather descending confidence; Wind-exposed climbing practice — simulate Jaizkibel conditions

### Iowa Wind and Rock
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 340 mi | 8,000 ft | Winterset, Iowa | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/iowa-wind-and-rock/

340 miles of Iowa gravel where the wind never stops and the suffering compounds every hour you're out there. Iowa Wind and Rock is 340 miles of honest Iowa gravel: crushed limestone roads rolling through endless agricultural land with zero shelter from wind. The terrain itself is moderate—8,000 feet of elevation spread thin via constant short climbs—but the format is brutal. You'll ride 30-50 hours continuously, crossing into overnight hours (often two nights for slower riders), navigating independently with GPS, and resupplying at small-town gas stations. The wind is THE defining feature: 10-20 mph sustained headwinds and crosswinds for hours. The constant rolling terrain means you never truly rest—always climbing or grinding into wind. River valley crossings add scenic variety and punchy elevation. The self-supported format strips away all external motivation. No crowds. No aid stations. No teammates. Just you, Iowa backroads, and the mental test of keeping pedals turning for 40+ hours when quitting would be easy. Signature challenge: Managing 30-50 hours of continuous riding while fighting relentless Iowa wind, sleep deprivation, and the psychological isolation of self-supported ultra-endurance racing with no external motivation. Iowa Wind and Rock is 340 miles of truth serum on gravel. You can't hide behind teammates, tactics, or short bursts of power. This is 30-50 hours of continuous forward motion where the wind grinds you down, the constant rolling terrain never lets you recover, and your own brain becomes the biggest obstacle. The terrain itself isn't hard—Iowa gravel is fast when dry, and there are no mountains. But the wind is relentless. You'll spend hours grinding into headwinds at 8-10 mph, watching your finish time balloon, questioning why you signed up. Sleep deprivation hits around Hour 25-30. Navigation errors happen. The loneliness of self-supported racing amplifies every doubt. And April weather is a lottery: rain turns the course into mud hell, cold overnight temps test your layering strategy, or you luck out with mild sunny conditions. If you finish, you'll have earned something rare: proof that you can keep going when every external factor and internal voice says stop. If you DNF, you'll learn exactly where your limits are. Either outcome teaches you something. You need 200+ mile experience and the mental fortitude to grind through 40+ hours of constant wind exposure across endless farm roads. This isn't about watts—it's about navigation skills, self-sufficiency, and accepting that you'll question every life choice around Mile 200. Skip it if you've never done overnight riding or think Iowa looks flat and easy.

### Lake Taupō Cycle Challenge
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 99.4 mi | 6,407 ft | Taupō, Waikato, New Zealand | November | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/lake-taupo-cycle-challenge/

New Zealand's biggest cycling event. 7,000 riders circling a supervolcano's caldera. 47 years of one schoolteacher's idea. A circumnavigation, not a criterium. The 160 km circuit follows state highways around Lake Taupō — New Zealand's largest lake and the caldera of the Taupō supervolcano. The terrain is rolling rather than mountainous, with three named hills providing structure: Waihaha (the false summit), Waihi (the big grind), and Hatepe (the late-course sting). The route passes through geothermal steam fields, rolling Waikato farmland, quiet southern-shore settlements, and the crystal-clear eastern lakeside. On a clear day, the volcanic peaks of Tongariro, Ngauruhoe, and Ruapehu dominate the southern horizon. The course rewards steady pacing over 5-8 hours, not explosive power. Signature challenge: The three hills — Waihaha, Waihi, and Hatepe — are the spine of the course. None of them are Alpine monsters, but each has its character: Waihaha lies with its false summit, Waihi demands sustained effort for the best views of the day, and Hatepe arrives when your legs have already given 120 km of effort. The real challenge is the totality: 160 km, 1,953 metres of climbing, potentially exposed to wind and rain, on a course that never lets you fully rest. The Lake Taupō Cycle Challenge is not a race. It is a pilgrimage. Every last Saturday of November since 1977, thousands of New Zealanders have driven to a small volcanic-plateau town to circle a supervolcano's caldera on their bicycles, because a schoolteacher named Walter de Bont once thought it would be a good idea to ride around the lake with 26 of his mates. That ride became New Zealand's largest cycling event — peaking at 10,000 riders in its heyday and still drawing 7,000 annually after 47 editions. The 160 km circuit takes in geothermal steam fields, rolling Waikato farmland, three named hills that each provide their own variety of suffering, and the crystal-clear eastern shore of the largest lake in New Zealand. On a clear day, you ride toward the snow-capped volcanic peaks of Tongariro and Ruapehu. On a bad day, you ride into wind and rain on exposed state highways. Either way, you finish in a town that has been celebrating your ride all day long. Yes if you want an authentic New Zealand cycling experience with volcanic scenery and genuine community atmosphere. Yes if you want a well-supported century ride (or 200-mile ultra) in the Southern Hemisphere. Skip if you need serious competition — this is a community ride, not a race. Skip if geographic isolation is a dealbreaker — getting to Taupō requires commitment. Non-negotiables: Century-distance endurance — comfortable riding 160 km (100 mi) with 2,000m climbing in a single day; Self-sufficient nutrition strategy tested over 5+ hours; Wind and weather resilience — ability to ride exposed roads in changing conditions

### L'Etape Brasil by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 66 mi | 7,546 ft | Campos do Jordao, Sao Paulo State, Brazil (flagship stage; series also visits Cunha and Rio de Janeiro) | September | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-brazil/

Latin America's biggest amateur cycling event: ASO-organized, closed-road mountain racing through Brazil's Serra da Mantiqueira with Tour de France classification jerseys and 3,500 riders. Mountain road course through the Serra da Mantiqueira -- Brazil's 'Swiss Alps.' The 107 km long course packs 2,300m of climbing into a compact route that winds through pine forests, mountain ridges, and the scenic countryside surrounding Campos do Jordao. The terrain is relentlessly hilly: there is very little flat riding between the climbs. Gradients alternate between sustained 5-7% drags and punchy 10%+ ramps. The course starts and finishes in the Capivari district, creating a natural amphitheater for the start/finish spectacle. Signature challenge: The accumulated climbing density: 2,300m of elevation gain packed into 107 km means you are climbing for approximately 60% of the race. There is no extended flat recovery between climbs. Every descent transitions directly into the next ascent. The compact, relentless nature of the climbing separates this from longer, flatter gran fondos. L'Etape Brasil brings ASO's Tour de France franchise to South America with impressive execution -- 107 km of closed-road mountain climbing through the Serra da Mantiqueira, classification jerseys, Shimano support, and 3,500+ riders chasing yellow in the 'Brazilian Switzerland.' Now in its second decade, this is the largest amateur cycling event in Latin America, and it runs with the professional-grade infrastructure you expect from the Tour de France brand. Campos do Jordao delivers 2,300m of genuine climbing in a charming mountain resort town where dawn starts at 5C give way to warm afternoon celebrations. The Brazilian cycling community brings infectious energy that makes the event experience genuinely special. The limitation is scale and context: this is a franchise event, not the original, and the 107 km course is shorter than the French edition's 170 km. The competitive field is strong domestically but thin internationally. What it does brilliantly is bring world-class cycling event production to a growing market that deserves it. Yes if you want Tour de France atmosphere at a fraction of European pricing. Yes if you are based in South America and want the most professionally organized sportive on the continent. Yes if 2,300m of mountain climbing on closed roads through stunning scenery sounds like your kind of weekend. Skip if you want the prestige of the French original -- this is a franchise, not the main event. Skip if you need deep international competition -- the field is overwhelmingly domestic. Non-negotiables: Sustained climbing ability -- multiple 20-40 minute climbs at threshold in a single ride; Cold-start preparation and layering strategy; Nutrition plan tested over 4-6 hours of mountain riding

### L'Etape Chile by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 68.3 mi | 3,937 ft | Valdivia, Los Rios, Chile | November | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-chile/

TdF magic meets Chilean wilderness—worth the trek! Coastal launch from Niebla rockets into Valdivia's emerald valleys. Rollers twist through ancient forests, rivers flashing beside. Gentle climbs build to panoramic payoffs—no endless grinds, all scenic reward. TdF signage elevates every pedal. Signature challenge: The Niebla-to-Valdivia coastal traverse: 110km essence in rainforest-wrapped rollers. 1,200m spread thin but relentless, with humidity amplifying burn. TdF finish line in Valdivia seals the conquest. L'Etape Chile blasts Tour spirit into Chile's lush south, blending coastal beauty with manageable suffering. Debut 110km from Niebla immerses you in rainforest rollers under Tour branding—no French Alps brutality, but fresh adventure. Skeptics yawn at low vert; purists drool over novelty. Valdivia's charm post-ride rivals any podium. Yes if craving exotic road prestige without Alpine agony. Skip if chasing mega-vert or elite competition. Non-negotiables: 8-10hr endurance base with 3,000m weekly vert; Pack rain kit and arm warmers

### L'Etape Italy Piemonte by Tour de France
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 31 mi | 2,608 ft | Entracque, Cuneo, Piemonte, Italy | May | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-italy-piemonte/

A charming, inclusive Tour de France experience that sacrifices competitive challenge for community and accessibility. L'Etape Piemonte is a scenic cycle-touring loop designed for recreational enjoyment rather than competitive racing. The route winds through Valle Gesso's varied alpine landscapes—forests, open valleys, and historic settlements—with paved provincial roads and steady, manageable gradients. The course prioritizes visual and cultural immersion over physical suffering, passing through charming villages like Valdieri, Borgo San Dalmazzo, and Roccavione. The 795 meters of climbing is distributed evenly across the 50 km, creating a rhythm of gentle ascent and descent rather than dramatic Alpine suffering. This is deliberately beginner-friendly terrain that welcomes e-bikes and families while remaining scenically rewarding. Signature challenge: The signature challenge is not a single dramatic climb but rather the sustained, steady climbing across the entire 50 km loop. With 795 meters distributed evenly, riders face consistent 5-7% gradients rather than a single punishing ascent. The real challenge is maintaining pace and enjoying the scenery simultaneously—a mental and physical balance rather than a pure suffering test. L'Etape Italy Piemonte is a masterclass in inclusive event design—it brings Tour de France atmosphere to recreational cyclists without pretense or gatekeeping. The 31-mile loop through Valle Gesso is genuinely scenic, the €25 entry fee is absurdly fair, and the community focus is refreshing in a cycling world obsessed with racing credentials. However, serious cyclists will find it underwhelming: the distance is short, the climbing is moderate, and the terrain is beginner-friendly. This is deliberately not a granfondo for racers; it's a celebration of cycling culture for families, e-bike users, and casual touring enthusiasts. If you're seeking a competitive test or serious Alpine suffering, look elsewhere. If you want to experience Tour de France magic without the suffering, this inaugural 2026 edition delivers. Yes if you're a recreational cyclist, family, or e-bike user seeking Tour de France atmosphere without suffering. Skip if you're a serious racer wanting Alpine challenge—the 31 miles and 2,608 feet of climbing won't test you. Non-negotiables: Basic fitness for 31 miles with 2,608 feet of climbing; Bike maintenance and mechanical competency; Layered clothing and weather preparation

### L'Étape Romania
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 50 mi | 500 ft | Bucharest, Romania | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/letape-romania/

A festive, accessible urban cycling celebration in Eastern Europe's premier amateur Tour de France event. Urban loop through Bucharest's historic heart on fully closed roads; celebratory, accessible, and landmark-rich rather than technically demanding Signature challenge: Maintaining momentum through dense urban sections and navigating 2,500+ cyclists on closed roads; psychological challenge of distance (85 km) rather than terrain difficulty Non-negotiables: Helmet required (standard cycling safety); Bike must be in safe working condition (brakes, tires, drivetrain functional); Age requirement: 15+ for 85 km and 42 km races; 8+ for 14 km City Adventure

### London to Brighton Bike Ride
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 54 mi | 2,500 ft | London to Brighton, England, UK | June | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/london-to-brighton/

54 miles from London to the sea. The ride that defined mass-participation charity cycling. Ditchling Beacon is the final exam. A 54-mile journey from the heart of London to the English seaside, crossing four counties and climbing over the South Downs. The route begins in the urban bustle of Clapham Common, transitions through South London's suburbs, and quickly enters the green countryside of Surrey. Rolling country lanes through the Weald of Sussex provide the middle miles — undulating terrain through small villages, past farms, and along hedgerow-lined roads. The final 10 miles deliver the ride's dramatic crescendo: the approach to Ditchling Beacon across open downland, the brutal 1-mile climb to the summit of the South Downs, the breathtaking view of the sea from the top, and the fast, euphoric descent into Brighton to finish on Madeira Drive along the seafront. Signature challenge: Ditchling Beacon — 1 mile at 9% average with a 16% maximum gradient. The most famous climb in southern England, tackled by 15,000+ riders every June. Spectators cheering, riders walking, and the view of the sea from the top. This single mile defines the entire ride. The London to Brighton Bike Ride did not invent cycling from a capital city to the coast. But it did more than any other event to establish mass-participation charity cycling as a concept that millions of people now take for granted. Since 1976, over 650,000 riders have left Clapham Common on a Sunday morning in June, rolled through the green lanes of Surrey and Sussex, dragged themselves up Ditchling Beacon, and descended in triumph to Brighton seafront. Fifty million pounds raised for the British Heart Foundation. One climb that has become the most famous in southern England. And a finish on the seafront that feels like every good thing that cycling can deliver — exhaustion, achievement, community, and the sea. This is not a race — it is a rite of passage. Yes if you want to experience the most iconic one-day charity cycling event in Europe. Yes if Ditchling Beacon is on your bucket list. Yes if you believe that 15,000 people sharing a road and a purpose is what cycling should feel like. Skip if you need timing chips, competition, or serious distance. The 54 miles will not test a strong cyclist's fitness — but the experience will stay with you regardless. Non-negotiables: Complete at least one 50+ mile training ride including sustained hill efforts; Practice riding in a group on narrow roads; Plan your return journey from Brighton before the ride

### Ned Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 75 mi | 6,000 ft | Nederland, Colorado | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/ned-gravel/

High-altitude Front Range gravel with ghost towns and serious climbing. High-altitude mountain gravel with significant climbing through ghost towns. Signature challenge: High-altitude mountain climbing in Colorado Rockies through ghost towns. Ned Gravel is a high-altitude mountain gravel race in Colorado's Front Range with significant climbing and thin air. Nederland sits at 8,000+ feet, and the course climbs to 10,300 feet through ghost towns and mining country. The Colorado Rockies setting and altitude challenge elevate it above typical regional events, but the field remains mostly regional. Near Boulder with moderate access. Well-organized with four distance options. If you're in Colorado and want a mountain gravel challenge near Boulder with real altitude—yes, it's a solid event. If you're from out of state—there are more prestigious options. Non-negotiables: Altitude acclimatization; Mountain climbing endurance; Mountain weather preparation

### Gran Fondo Pekan / Pekan Classic

Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/pekan-classics/

Scenic, affordable UCI gran fondo blending flat speed with moderate Pahang hills. Flat river-hugging out-and-back with a central loop deviation, featuring moderate rolling hills and short steep pitches amid tropical scenery. Signature challenge: Pahang River double-sided routing: 108km flat borders punctuated by a hilly loop for 2500ft gain.

### Pelotonia
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 100 mi | 2,500 ft | Columbus, Ohio, USA | August | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/pelotonia/

$339 million raised. 7,500 riders. 100% of every dollar to cancer research. The terrain is Ohio. The mission is everything. A point-to-point ride from downtown Columbus across Central Ohio's gently rolling farmland. The route leaves the urban grid within the first 10 miles, transitioning through suburbs and into genuinely rural countryside — corn and soybean fields, white farmhouses, quiet county roads lined with hardwood trees. The terrain is a series of short rollers, none individually significant but cumulatively honest over 100 miles in August heat. The 100-mile route culminates at Kenyon College in Gambier — a hilltop liberal arts campus that transforms into a finish line celebration. The 165-mile two-day option adds a Sunday return from Kenyon through different countryside to New Albany. Signature challenge: The heat. Not the terrain, not the distance, not the climbing — the August Ohio heat and humidity that turns an otherwise moderate century into a genuine test of preparation and resilience. Every experienced Pelotonia rider will tell you the same thing: respect the weather. Pelotonia is the rare cycling event where the suffering has a point beyond personal satisfaction. $339 million raised for cancer research — every dollar from riders going directly to the Ohio State Comprehensive Cancer Center, operational costs covered by corporate partners so nothing leaks to overhead. The riding itself is honest but unremarkable: flat-to-rolling Ohio farmland in August heat, paved roads, no technical demands. What makes Pelotonia extraordinary is the community — 7,500 riders who have raised thousands of dollars each, many riding for deeply personal reasons, united by a single goal that is bigger than any of them. The Opening Ceremony will give you chills. The finish line might make you cry. The terrain will not. Yes if you want to be part of one of the most impactful cycling communities in America. Yes if cancer has touched your life and you want to ride with meaning. Yes if you value community and mission over scenery and competition. Skip if you cannot commit to the fundraising requirement, need competitive racing, or want dramatic terrain. Non-negotiables: Heat acclimatization training — train in heat and humidity before August; Hydration and electrolyte strategy tested on rides of 60+ miles; Fundraising commitment fulfilled before ride weekend

### Tour of Oman Sportive
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 105 mi | 8,500 ft | Muscat, Oman | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-of-oman-sportive/

Pro roads, Arabian climbs: winter sportive gold.

### USA Cycling Gravel Nationals
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 131 mi | 7,000 ft | La Crescent, Minnesota (2025-26); previously Gering, Nebraska (2023-24) | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/usa-cycling-gravel-nationals/

The national championship with open entry—stars and stripes on Driftless gravel with UCI Worlds pathway. Driftless Region 'champagne gravel' with punchy blufftop climbs. The terrain looks Midwestern-gentle but delivers relentless 10-15% gradients throughout. Apple orchards, gravel quarries, and river valley views provide scenery between efforts. Signature challenge: The relentless punchy climbs—not big sustained efforts, but repeated 10-15% gradients that accumulate suffering throughout the championship distance. USA Cycling Gravel Nationals delivers what the name promises—a legitimate national championship with open entry and UCI Worlds qualification. The Driftless Region course is harder than it looks with punchy 10-15% climbs and 'champagne gravel' that gets loose when freshly graded. Young event (est. 2023) finding its identity, but the jersey means something. Yes if you want a legitimate national championship jersey and Worlds qualification. Yes if you appreciate accessible championship racing. Skip if you want destination adventure over competitive credentialing.

125-mile high-altitude Colorado mountain gravel in Leadville. High-altitude mountain gravel for 125 miles. Vapor Trail 125 is a 125-mile high-altitude gravel race in Leadville, Colorado—mountain terrain with extreme altitude (10,000ft+). The combination of 125 miles and extreme altitude is a serious challenge, but the moderate organization and regional field keep it in Tier 3. Leadville provides moderate access. ### Boulder Roubaix Road Race
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 30 mi | 1,200 ft | Boulder Reservoir, Boulder County, Colorado, USA | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/boulder-roubaix/

Colorado's 34-year spring classic. 61% dirt, USAC-sanctioned, and unapologetically hard. Rolling mixed-surface through North Boulder County agricultural landscape. The 2026 course is a new 30-mile (48 km) loop — 61% unpaved, 39% paved — that starts and finishes at Boulder Reservoir. The terrain is constant undulation through working farm and county roads that can be anywhere from smooth to extremely rutted depending on weather. No single dramatic climb, but the accumulated rolling and the wind across open terrain create a war of attrition. The finishing stretch along the Boulder Feeder Canal and across the reservoir dams adds a challenging technical element to the final miles. Signature challenge: The wind. Not the terrain, not the climbing, not the distance — the wind across open North Boulder County farmland is what defines Boulder Roubaix. In 2024, it shut down the race. On any given edition, it determines whether the dirt roads are fast-rolling or soul-crushing. Combined with the unpredictable surface conditions and spring weather variability, the wind transforms a rolling 30-mile circuit into a legitimate test of tactical racing and survival. Boulder Roubaix is what happens when you take the spirit of Paris-Roubaix and transplant it to Colorado's Front Range farm roads. Since 1992, this biennial race has sent riders onto the unpaved county roads north of Boulder — roads that were 'gravel' decades before gravel became a category. The course does not need cobblestones to create chaos: rutted dirt roads, spring wind that can shut down the race, and surface conditions that change hourly deliver the unpredictability that defines a classic. It is a proper USAC road race with categorized fields and a pro purse, not a recreational ride. The 2026 edition moves to Boulder Reservoir with a new 30-mile loop that is 61% unpaved — further embracing the mixed-surface identity that has always defined this event. Yes if you are a competitive road or gravel racer who wants a legitimate USAC road race on mixed surfaces. Yes if you live in Colorado and want to test your early-season form on the defining spring race. Yes if you want to experience the event that was doing 'gravel racing' before the term existed. Skip if you want a long-distance endurance challenge (most categories are 30-60 miles), or if you need a destination event with a national profile. Non-negotiables: Mixed-surface tire selection tested on dirt roads; Wind-exposure training on open terrain; Self-sufficiency kit: pump, spare tube, multi-tool

### Cape Rouleur
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 373 mi | 19,685 ft | Franschhoek, Western Cape, South Africa | March | road
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/cape-rouleur/

600 km through the Cape Winelands on closed roads with pro support. Cycling's most luxurious sportive experience. The Cape Rouleur unfolds across five stages radiating from Franschhoek into the Western Cape's finest cycling terrain. Each stage delivers a different character: wine country rolling roads through Stellenbosch, mountain pass climbs through the Hottentots Holland range, coastal sections with views of False Bay, and the dramatic landscapes around Paarl and Wellington. The riding is demanding — 120 km per day average with 1,200m climbing — but the closed roads and support infrastructure remove every variable except your legs and the scenery. The final day typically includes an approach into Cape Town, setting up the optional Cape Town Cycle Tour. Signature challenge: The queen stage mountain passes. After three prior stages of cumulative fatigue, the most demanding climbing of the week arrives. The Western Cape's mountain passes are not Alpine monsters, but at 30C with the Cape Doctor blowing and four days of riding in the legs, 1,500m of climbing in a single stage is absolutely honest. The Cape Rouleur is what happens when you give 200 amateur cyclists the professional treatment in one of the world's most beautiful cycling destinations. HotChillee's formula is proven: closed roads, motorbike outriders, lead car nutrition, mechanics with spare wheels, a race doctor, and a broom wagon. You don't think about traffic, navigation, or mechanical issues. You just ride.

The setting makes it sing. Five stages through the Cape Winelands — Franschhoek, Stellenbosch, and the surrounding wine country — deliver vineyards, mountain passes, and coastal roads in succession. The hospitality in Franschhoek between stages is exceptional. Wine tastings, gourmet South African food, poolside recovery. This is cycling as luxury experience.

The price reflects the quality: GBP 1,350+ entry is steep, and flights to Cape Town add another GBP 500-1,200. But for the cycling tourist who wants five days of world-class riding with zero logistics burden, there is nothing better. Yes if you want a premium multi-stage cycling holiday with world-class support in a stunning setting. Yes if the combination of serious daily riding and Franschhoek wine country hospitality appeals to your inner cycling tourist. Skip if the GBP 3,000-4,000 total cost is prohibitive. Skip if you want a competitive event with timing and rankings. Non-negotiables: Multi-day block training — 5 consecutive days of 100+ km rides to simulate stage race fatigue; Heat acclimatization — riding in warm conditions (25C+) for extended periods; Wind riding practice — headwind and crosswind group riding skills

Cohutta Gravel Grinder is a 100-mile mountain gravel race through north Georgia's Cohutta Wilderness—significant climbing with wilderness scenery. The organization is solid, and the Georgia reputation is decent. However, it's a regional field, keeping it in Tier 3. Chatsworth provides moderate access.
